name: Chorizo & Mozzarella Arancini
subtitle: Lemon & Thyme Aioli · Romesco
label: Arancini - Large

ingredients.prep: plus extra for seasoning at fry

method_steps: Heat a splash of oil in a large rondeau over medium heat on the 6-burner. Sauté blitzed brown onion, stirring frequently, for 8–10 minutes until golden and soft. Add crushed garlic and cook 1 minute until fragrant. Add finely diced chorizo and cook a further 3–4 minutes until beginning to colour and fat has rendered.
Deglaze with white wine and reduce by half, approximately 3–4 minutes.
Add Arborio rice and stir to coat in the chorizo fat for 1–2 minutes. Add spicy capsicum dip, water and chicken stock concentrate. Season with salt and cracked pepper. Stir to combine thoroughly.

Transfer mixture to a deep gastronorm bain. Cover tightly with foil or lid. Bake in conventional oven at 160°C for 20 minutes. Remove, stir thoroughly, re-cover and bake a further 20–25 minutes until all liquid is absorbed and rice is fully cooked but not mushy.

Spread cooked rice evenly across two shallow bains to a depth of no more than 5cm. Cool uncovered for 15 minutes, then refrigerate until core temperature reaches 5°C or below. Do not skip this step — rolling warm rice produces soft balls that won't hold structure.
Once fully chilled, fold in all three grated cheeses and the blitzed roasted capsicum. Mix until evenly incorporated. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Weigh rice into 100g portions (not 50g — yield is 80 × 100g as per recipe). Roll firmly into tight balls, compressing well so there are no air pockets.

Set up a three-stage coating station: (1) GF flour, (2) beaten egg whisked with cold water (no milk), (3) GF breadcrumbs. Roll each ball in flour first — shake off excess. Dip in egg wash ensuring full coverage. Roll firmly in breadcrumbs, pressing to adhere. Double-crumb if any bald patches appear.
Arrange coated balls on parchment-lined trays without touching. Freeze until completely solid, minimum 4 hours or overnight.

Once frozen solid, portion into labelled vacuum bags. SEAL ONLY — do not apply vacuum pressure as this will crush the balls. Store in freezer crate labelled ARANCINI. Defrost in coldroom for minimum 24 hours before service.
service: Preheat fryer oil to 180°C. Do not fry from frozen — ensure arancini are fully defrosted and at fridge temperature before frying.

Fry in small batches of 6–8, do not overcrowd. Fry 4–5 minutes, turning once, until deep golden brown and heated through. Check with a metal skewer — hold against your wrist, it should feel hot after 3 seconds.
Drain on chux-lined tray. Season immediately with salt flakes while hot.
Warm Romesco in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring, or microwave in 30-second bursts until hot through.
Spoon 60–80ml warm Romesco as a base on each plate or slate. Arrange 3 arancini on top.
Dot a small amount of Lemon Thyme Aioli on each arancini. Garnish with freshly picked microherbs. Serve immediately.
note: DO NOT vacuum seal — seal only. Defrost minimum 24 hours in coldroom before frying. Frying from frozen will result in a cold centre and over-coloured crust. Double-crumbing is recommended for canapé service durability.

name: Calamari Fritti
subtitle: Lemon Dill Aioli · Crispy Capers
label: Calamari

ingredients.prep: plus extra for seasoning at service

method_steps: STORAGE: When calamari and octopus arrive, ensure fully defrosted before portioning. Portion into 500ml PCs — 15 pieces calamari + 3 baby octopus per PC. Pat dry before storing to minimise excess moisture. Label with date and item name, store in freezer crate labelled CALAMARI.

FLOUR MIX: Combine rice flour, GF corn flour, fine salt, cracked pepper and lemon pepper seasoning in a large bowl. Whisk thoroughly to distribute seasoning evenly. Transfer to labelled PCs or a sealed container. Store in coldroom — keeping it cold prevents moisture uptake and keeps the coating crisp.

CAPERS: Drain thoroughly in a sieve. Spread onto chux and press firmly to remove as much moisture as possible — wet capers will spit aggressively in hot oil and won't crisp. Store in a PC lined with fresh chux, refrigerate. Do not season until after frying.
service: Preheat fryer oil to 190°C. A higher temperature ensures capers and calamari crisp quickly without absorbing excess oil.

Fry capers in a dry basket (no coating) for 45–60 seconds until they open out and turn deep golden. Remove immediately and drain on chux-lined bowl. They will crisp further as they cool. Do not cover.
Working in batches, toss calamari strips and baby octopus in flour mix — coat evenly and shake off all excess flour before frying. Excess flour drops into the oil and burns.
Fry immediately in batches — do not crowd the basket. Calamari: 60–90 seconds until golden and just cooked through. Baby octopus: 90 seconds to 2 minutes. Drain on chux-lined tray.
Season with a pinch of fine salt immediately while hot.
TAPAS: Spoon 30–40ml lemon dill aioli as a base on the plate. Arrange calamari and baby octopus on top. Scatter crispy capers and finish with microgreens.
CANAPE: Small dot of aioli in bamboo boat. Calamari on top, 2–3 crispy capers to finish.
note: Calamari and octopus must be completely dry before coating — surface moisture creates steam in the fryer, producing a soggy crust. Pat dry before storing and again before coating at service. Oil temperature is critical: below 180°C the coating absorbs oil and goes limp.

name: Kilpatrick Oysters
subtitle: Crispy Speck · Lea & Perrins · Rock Salt
label: Oysters Kilpatrick

ingredients.prep: defrost 24 hrs in coldroom, drain and pat dry

method_steps: OYSTERS: Remove from freezer 24 hours before the event and defrost in the coldroom. Once defrosted, drain any liquid and pat the oyster meat gently dry with chux. Keep refrigerated until service.
SPECK: Finely dice speck to 3–4mm. Store in a labelled PC in the coldroom. The smaller dice renders quickly under oven heat and distributes evenly across the oyster.

KILPATRICK SAUCE: Combine GF Worcestershire sauce, BBQ sauce, Tabasco and dried parsley. Whisk to combine. Taste — sauce should be savoury, slightly smoky and have a clean chilli back-note. Adjust Tabasco if required. Decant into a labelled squeezy bottle. Refrigerate.
GARNISH: Slice lemons into 5mm rounds. Store in a labelled PC in the coldroom.
service: Preheat conventional oven to 200°C fan-forced (or 210°C conventional). A high heat ensures the speck renders and caramelises quickly without overcooking the oyster.
Scrunch aluminium foil into a deep bain tray, creating an uneven surface that holds oyster shells stable and level. This prevents the sauce from running off during baking.
Arrange oysters on the foil-lined tray. Spoon 5–8ml Kilpatrick sauce directly onto each oyster. Top with 1 heaped teaspoon diced speck per oyster.

Bake at 200°C for 6–8 minutes until the speck is golden and beginning to caramelise at the edges and the oyster is just warmed through. Do not overbake — the oyster should still be plump and just set, not shrunken.
Spread rock salt 2cm thick on the serving tray or plate to stabilise shells. Nestle baked oysters into the salt. Garnish with rocket or microgreens and lemon rounds. Serve immediately.
note: Do not overbake. Oysters continue to carry-cook after leaving the oven. Target internal temperature of 65°C — the meat should be just opaque and plump. Shrunken, tight oyster meat means they are overcooked. Kilpatrick sauce can be made 3 days in advance and stored refrigerated in squeezy bottles.

name: Polpette
subtitle: Beef & Pork · Romesco Sugo · Focaccia · Gremolata
label: Polpette

ingredients.prep: for crumbing coat — keep separate from panade crumbs
whisked with a splash of cold water, for egg wash — crumbing station
house-made — needed for baking; have ready before cook stage

method_steps: PANADE: Place 800 g GF breadcrumbs in a large deep container. Pour all 2.5 kg full-cream milk over the breadcrumbs. Press down to submerge and rest for 15–20 minutes until the breadcrumbs have fully absorbed the milk and become a thick, wet paste. Do not squeeze out the milk — the full soak is intentional and gives the polpette their moisture and tenderness.

ONION & GARLIC: Sweat 500 g finely diced onion and 200 g crushed garlic in a splash of oil over medium heat on the 6-burner for 8–10 minutes until fully soft and translucent. Do not colour. Cool completely before adding to the mix.

COMBINE: In a large mixing bin, combine 4.5 kg beef mince, 3.5 kg pork mince, the full soaked panade, cooled onion and garlic, stripped thyme leaves, 1 kg blitzed parmesan, 18 eggs, 250 g salt and 125 g cracked pepper. Mix by hand until just combined — do not overwork. The mix should feel moist, cohesive and slightly tacky. Overworking develops the proteins and produces a tight, rubbery texture.
TASTE TEST: Roll a 20 g test ball, flatten into a patty and pan-fry until cooked through. Taste for salt, pepper and herb balance. Adjust the full batch before rolling.

ROLL: Wet hands to prevent sticking. Weigh and roll into 60 g balls. Press firmly so there are no surface cracks or air pockets. Place on lined trays as you go — do not stack. Rest in fridge for 15 minutes before crumbing.

CRUMB: Set up a crumbing station with a tray of 500 g GF breadcrumbs and 6 whisked eggs for egg wash. Working in batches, dip each 60 g ball in egg wash — eggs whisked with cold water, no milk (let excess drip off), then roll in GF breadcrumbs to coat evenly. Press lightly to ensure the crumb adheres with no bare patches. Return crumbed balls to lined trays in a single layer. Keep cold between batches.

BAKE: Ladle enough romesco sugo into deep gastronorm bains to cover the base generously. Arrange crumbed polpette in a single layer — do not stack. Ladle more romesco over the top so each ball is at least half submerged. Cover bains tightly with foil. Steam-bake at 160°C (steam setting) for 30 minutes total — remove foil at 15 minutes, carefully flip each ball, re-cover and return for the remaining 15 minutes. Internal temperature must reach 72°C at the centre. The crumb will absorb romesco and become tender — this is correct.
COOL & STORE: Allow to cool in the sauce. Once below 20°C, portion into PCs in serves of 3 with sauce distributed evenly. Label with name and date. Freeze in crate labelled POLPETTE.
service: Reheat portioned polpette and sauce in the microwave in 60-second bursts until steaming throughout. Check internal temperature — minimum 75°C before service.
Transfer to a medium serving bowl. Add extra warmed romesco if portions look dry.
Toast 3 slices of focaccia (2 cm thick) on the kick oven or flat grill until golden on both sides.
Top polpette with a generous pinch of freshly made gremolata and fine-grated Manchego. Arrange focaccia alongside. Serve immediately.
note: Beef and pork mince replaces the original veal — the 56/44 split gives depth from the beef and fat/sweetness from the pork. Panade is the full 2.5 kg milk — do not reduce or squeeze out. The wet panade is what gives these polpette their moisture and tenderness. Do not overwork the mix after combining. Balls are rolled at 60 g (pre-crumb weight) then crumbed in GF breadcrumbs for a crisp exterior. Keep mix cold throughout. Prep time approximately 75–90 minutes per full batch.

name: Lemon Pepper Chicken Skewer
subtitle: Herbed Labneh · Crumbled Feta · Microherbs
label: Chicken Skewers

ingredients.prep: for labneh — adds brightness in place of raw cucumber

method_steps: MARINADE: Combine olive oil, lemon pepper seasoning, limoncello, lemon juice and zest, honey or castor sugar, and picked thyme leaves. Whisk to combine. Taste — marinade should be bright, aromatic and well-seasoned.
Dice chicken thigh into 3cm pieces. Add to marinade and toss to coat evenly. Cover and refrigerate for a minimum of 4 hours, ideally overnight in the coldroom.

Thread 3 pieces of chicken per 12cm wooden skewer, leaving a small gap between pieces for even heat penetration. Store in 500ml PCs (3 skewers per serve). Label and freeze in crate labelled CHICKEN SKEWERS.

LABNEH: Line a perforated gastronorm or colander with 3 layers of chux (or a clean muslin cloth). Pour yogurt in and fold the cloth over the top. Hang over a deep bain to collect whey. Refrigerate overnight — minimum 12 hours. The labneh is ready when it is thick, creamy and holds its shape.

KUSCHI EXCLUSION — RAW CUCUMBER: Raw cucumber is not used at Kuschi's Kitchen. Instead, fold 30ml pickled cucumber brine into the hung labneh — this adds the same brightness and acidity without raw cucumber. Alternatively, simply increase the quantity of fresh dill for herbaceous freshness.

Mix hung labneh with finely chopped parsley, dill, mint, lemon zest and pickled cucumber brine. Season with a squeeze of lemon juice, salt and cracked pepper. Taste and adjust. Refrigerate in a labelled PC.
service: Defrost skewers in coldroom for minimum 24 hours before service.
Preheat conventional oven to 200°C fan-forced. Line trays with baking paper.

Lay defrosted skewers flat on trays in a single layer — do not overlap. Roast for 12–15 minutes, turning once at the halfway point, until golden with some caramelisation on the edges and internal temperature reaches 75°C. Do not undercook — chicken thigh at service must be fully cooked through.
Spoon a heaped 40–50ml of herbed labneh onto the centre of each plate. Arrange 3 skewers on top. Crumble feta generously over and finish with green microherbs. Serve immediately.
note: RAW CUCUMBER IS A KUSCHI EXCLUSION — do not use in labneh or anywhere on this dish. Use pickled cucumber brine (30ml per 2kg yogurt batch) for brightness, or increase fresh dill. Chicken skewers must reach an internal temperature of 75°C before service. Do not roast from frozen.

name: Crispy Fried Chorizo Potatoes
subtitle: Lemon Thyme Aioli · Shallots
label: Chorizo Potatoes

ingredients.prep: sliced lengthwise then cut diagonally at 45° into 5mm pieces

method_steps: Wash potatoes. Bake whole, unpeeled, on oven trays at 200°C for 35–45 minutes until a skewer passes through with no resistance. Baking time will vary with potato size — check at 35 minutes. Do not underbake; a firm centre will result in dense, unpleasant smashed pieces.
Allow potatoes to cool until handleable, approximately 20–30 minutes at room temperature. Do not refrigerate before smashing — cold potatoes break unevenly and the skin does not smash cleanly.

Place each potato on a flat surface and press firmly with the heel of your hand or a flat-bottomed pan until it breaks into rough, uneven pieces. Aim for 6–8 jagged pieces per large potato — the irregular edges create maximum surface area for crispiness. Do not press into a flat disc.
Store smashed potato pieces in a single layer in labelled PCs in the coldroom. Do not stack — stacking causes the pieces to steam and go soggy. They must be cold and dry before frying.
Slice chorizo lengthwise down the centre, then cut each half diagonally at 45° into 5mm pieces. Store in a labelled PC in the coldroom.
Slice shallots thinly at 45°. Store separately in a labelled PC in the coldroom.
service: Preheat fryer oil to 185°C.
Fry chilled potato pieces in batches — do not overcrowd. Fry 3–4 minutes until deep golden brown and very crispy. The exterior should be crunchy and the inside fluffy. Drain on chux-lined tray.
In the final 30 seconds of the potato fry, add the chorizo slices to the basket. Fry 20–30 seconds until the edges begin to caramelise. Do not over-fry chorizo — it will tighten and become hard.

Transfer hot potato pieces and chorizo to a large metal bowl. Add a generous spoon of Lemon Thyme Aioli and sliced shallots. Season with salt flakes and cracked pepper. Toss to coat — the aioli should lightly dress everything, not pool at the bottom.
Serve 4–5 pieces in bamboo boats with a bamboo fork. Finish with an extra small dot of Lemon Thyme Aioli on top if desired.
note: Potato pieces must be fully chilled and dry before frying for maximum crispiness. Frying warm or damp potato produces a soft, oily crust. Aioli dressing should be light — toss and serve immediately, do not allow dressed potatoes to sit or they will go limp.

name: Chargrilled Lamb Cutlet
subtitle: Riviera House Emulsion · Rocket
label: Lamb Cutlets
method_steps: HOUSE LAMB CITRUS HERB BRINE: Combine 5L water, 180g salt and 120g sugar in a large pot. Heat over medium heat on the 6-burner, stirring until salt and sugar are fully dissolved. Remove from heat. Add smashed garlic cloves, rosemary sprigs, thyme sprigs and halved lemons. Allow brine to cool completely to below 5°C before use — never add meat to a warm brine.
French the cutlets, ensuring bones are clean. Place cutlets in a deep bain or suitable container in a single layer where possible.

Pour cooled brine over the cutlets — ensure all meat is fully submerged. Weigh down with a clean bain or plate if needed. Cover, label clearly with LAMB CUTLETS — BRINE DATE and the event date, and refrigerate in the coldroom for 24 hours. Do not exceed 24 hours as the lemon acidity will begin to break down the surface texture.

After 24 hours, remove cutlets from brine. Discard brine. Pat cutlets thoroughly dry with chux on all surfaces — dry meat is essential for a proper sear. Store uncovered or loosely covered on a rack in the coldroom until service to allow the surface to dry further.
Bring cutlets to room temperature for 20–30 minutes before cooking.
service: Preheat chargrill or a heavy cast iron pan on the 6-burner until very hot — a drop of water should evaporate immediately on contact.
Season brined cutlets lightly with salt flakes and cracked pepper — the brine has already seasoned the meat internally, so be restrained with surface salt.
Sear cutlets for 2 minutes per side on the chargrill or in the pan until deeply coloured with visible caramelisation. Work in batches — do not crowd the pan or the meat will steam rather than sear.
Transfer seared cutlets to a bain tray. Finish in the conventional oven or kick oven at 180°C for 6–8 minutes for medium (internal temperature 60–62°C). Adjust time for volume of covers.
Rest cutlets for 3–4 minutes before plating. This is non-negotiable at 80 covers — resting ensures the juices redistribute and the cutlet holds on the plate.

Lay a small bed of fresh rocket on the plate. Arrange 3 cutlets neatly on top, bones pointing upward. Drizzle Riviera House Emulsion generously over the cutlets, ensuring each is well-coated. Serve immediately.
note: Lamb cutlets are brined for 24 hours in the house Lamb Citrus Herb Brine (180g salt, 120g sugar, garlic, rosemary, thyme, lemon, 5L water) before cooking. Do NOT use the old 'season two days before' method — brine protocol replaces this entirely. Brine must be fully cold before meat is added. Pat cutlets completely dry after brining for an effective sear. Season lightly at service — the brine seasons from within.

name: Crispy Reef Fish Slider
subtitle: Lemon Caper Aioli · Rocket · Brioche
label: Fish Slider

ingredients.item: Reef Fish (firm white — snapper or coral trout)
ingredients.prep: portioned into 70 g pieces, patted dry, seasoned lightly with salt
for batter — improves crunch and reduces gluten snap
ice cold — keep on ice during service prep
picked, washed, dried — 3 g per slider
sufficient to fill fryer to fill line — approx. 15 L per fryer

method_steps: FISH PREP (2 days before): Transfer frozen fish to fridge for full 48-hour thaw. Do not thaw under running water — moisture ruins batter adhesion.

PORTION (day before): Pat fish completely dry with paper towel. Cut into 70 g portions. Season lightly with salt flakes. Place on wire rack over tray, uncovered, in coldroom for 1 hour to form a light pellicle — this is critical for batter adhesion.

BATTER (day before — prep base dry mix only): Combine 750 g plain flour, 250 g cornflour, 20 g garlic powder, 20 g onion powder, 15 g salt flakes and 10 g cracked white pepper in a large mixing bowl. Whisk dry to distribute evenly. Store sealed in a labelled container.

BATTER (day of — activate): Whisk 900 ml ice-cold sparkling water into dry mix in a slow stream. Mix until just combined — lumps are fine. Do not overwork. Fold in 30 ml vodka last. Batter should coat the back of a spoon cleanly. Keep bowl over ice throughout service prep.

PART-COOK (day before, fryer at 170°C): Dip each fish piece in batter, allow excess to drip 2 seconds, lower gently into fryer. Fry in batches of 6 maximum — do not crowd. Part-cook for 90 seconds until batter is just set and pale gold but fish is still raw in centre. Remove to wire rack. Cool fully before storing.
STORAGE: Lay part-cooked fish single layer on baking paper in bain-marie trays. Cover tightly with cling film, label with date and time. Refrigerate at or below 4°C. Use within 18 hours.

LEMON CAPER AIOLI: Combine 1 kg Kewpie mayo, 150 g chopped capers, zest of 3 lemons, 80 ml lemon juice, 20 g fresh dill. Season with 5 g salt flakes and 5 g white pepper. Mix well. Taste — should be bright, briny and rich. Refrigerate in labelled PC container. Yield approx. 1.1 kg — sufficient for 22 g per slider.
BUNS: Remove brioche buns from freezer 24 hours before service. Separate tops and bottoms. Store wrapped in cling film in fridge.
service: Preheat kick oven to 180°C. Place brioche buns cut-side up on wire rack tray. Bake 5–6 minutes until lightly toasted and warmed through. Do not over-toast — brioche burns fast. Hold wrapped in a clean tea towel.

Bring fryers to 180°C. Fry part-cooked fish in batches of 8 for 2–2.5 minutes until deep golden and internal temperature reaches 70°C. Season immediately with a pinch of salt flakes on exit from fryer. Hold on wire rack — never on paper towel, which steams and softens the crust. Service window: 4 minutes maximum from fryer to pass.

ASSEMBLY (two-person flow): Person 1 — place brioche base on platter, add 3 g rocket (small pinch), pipe or spoon 22 g lemon caper aioli onto rocket. Person 2 — place fish on aioli, top with brioche lid, press gently, secure with bamboo skewer at an angle.
Arrange 10 per platter, skewer-side up. Send immediately — do not hold assembled sliders.
note: Batter must be kept ice-cold throughout service — split into two bowls and rotate from fridge. Vodka in batter inhibits gluten development and flash-evaporates in the fryer, producing a lighter, crisper shell. Self-raising flour replaced with plain flour + cornflour for controlled leavening and superior texture. Aioli yield calculated at 22 g per slider with 10% buffer. Confirm reef fish species with supplier — coral trout preferred for firmness under high-heat fry.

name: Beef Kofta
subtitle: Spicy Capsicum Sauce · Pita · Rocket
label: Beef Kofta

ingredients.prep: minimum 20% fat — lean mince will produce dry, crumbling koftas
leaves only, finely chopped — approx. 40 g picked
leaves only, finely chopped — approx. 30 g picked
lightly beaten — binder to prevent koftas splitting on reheat
ready-made or house-made — room temperature for service
picked, washed, dried — 3 g per serve

method_steps: ONION & GARLIC: Heat 30 ml olive oil in a pan over medium heat on 6-burner. Sauté 300 g finely diced brown onion and 80 g crushed garlic until fully translucent and beginning to caramelise — approx. 8–10 minutes. Do not rush — raw onion in the mix will release moisture during baking and cause koftas to split. Cool completely before adding to mince.

SPICE MIX: Combine 20 g cumin, 20 g coriander, 10 g cinnamon, 15 g smoked paprika, 5 g cayenne, 40 g salt flakes, 15 g cracked pepper in a small bowl. Toast spice mix dry in a small pan for 60 seconds until fragrant. Cool before adding to mince.

MIX: In a large bowl combine 4 kg beef mince, cooled onion and garlic, toasted spice mix, 40 g chopped parsley, 30 g chopped mint and 2 beaten eggs. Mix by hand for 3–4 minutes until fully incorporated and the mixture becomes slightly sticky. This activates the myosin proteins and binds the kofta.
TASTE TEST: Fry a 15 g patty in a dry pan until cooked through. Taste for salt, heat and spice balance. Adjust before shaping all 50.

SHAPE: Using a 80 g portion scoop (yields 50 koftas from 4 kg mix), shape each portion into a finger-oval approximately 8 cm × 3 cm. Place on baking paper-lined bain-marie trays, single layer. Do not stack.
FREEZE: Open-freeze koftas on trays for 2 hours until firm. Vacuum seal in bags of 25. Label with name, date, yield. Return to freezer. Use within 6 weeks.
DEFROST: Transfer from freezer to coldroom 24 hours before service. Do not defrost at room temperature.
PITA: Defrost 50 mini pita breads in coldroom 24 hours before service. Store covered in bain-marie trays in fridge.
GARNISH HERBS: Finely chop additional parsley and mint. Combine 50/50. Store in a labelled PC container in coldroom. Use same day.
service: Preheat kick oven to 200°C (top and bottom heat, no fan). Place koftas on wire rack over bain-marie tray — this allows hot air circulation and prevents steaming on the base. Bake for 14–16 minutes until internal temperature reaches 75°C. Rest 3 minutes before service. Do not bake in a solid-base bain — the bottom will stew and lose texture.
Warm pita in kick oven at 180°C for 4 minutes, wrapped loosely in foil in batches. Hold wrapped in a clean towel — they dry out fast uncovered.

ASSEMBLY: Open pita at one end to create a pocket. Place 3 g rocket inside base. Add one kofta. Spoon 30 g spicy capsicum sauce over kofta. Finish with a pinch of fresh herb mix. Serve open-faced or pocket-style per event brief.
Arrange 10 per platter, alternating angles. Garnish platter edge with remaining herb mix. Send immediately — koftas lose texture if held more than 8 minutes post-oven.
note: Egg added as binder — critical for preventing split on reheating from frozen. Fat percentage in mince is non-negotiable at 20% minimum; extra-lean mince will produce dry, chalk-textured koftas. Spice quantities doubled from original spec — the original 10 g per spice across 4 kg mince was significantly under-seasoned. Taste test step is mandatory before shaping full batch.

name: Camembert, Pecan & Cranberry Cigars
subtitle: Thyme Honey · Microherbs
label: Camembert Cigars

ingredients.prep: cold from fridge — cut into 1 cm × 8 cm sticks, 14 g each. Must be cold to cut cleanly and hold shape during rolling.
blitzed to a rough crumb — no pieces larger than 4 mm
finely chopped by hand or in food processor
thawed in fridge overnight — do not thaw at room temperature, sheets crack
mild-flavoured — leatherwood or orange blossom recommended
leaves stripped from 2 sprigs for infusion; reserve 2 sprigs whole
for garnish — pick just before service
sufficient for fryer fill line — approx. 15 L per fryer

method_steps: CAMEMBERT PREP: Refrigerate camembert until firm — minimum 1 hour at 4°C. Cut into 1 cm × 8 cm sticks while cold. Season lightly with salt flakes and pepper. Lay on a tray lined with baking paper and return to fridge while preparing filo.

NOTE — GARLIC REMOVED: Crushed garlic has been removed from the original spec. Garlic does not belong in this flavour profile and competes directly with the thyme-honey finish. If a savoury note is needed, a pinch of smoked paprika on the filling is a better choice.
FILLING MIX: Combine 200 g pecan crumb and 200 g chopped cranberries. Season with a pinch of salt and pepper. Mix to combine. This is the filling spread — approx. 8 g per cigar.

FILO ROLLING: Work with one sheet of filo at a time. Keep remaining sheets under a lightly damp tea towel to prevent drying. Lay one sheet flat, brush lightly but evenly with melted butter. Lay a second sheet directly on top, butter again. Cut the double layer into strips approximately 10 cm wide × 20 cm long — each double-buttered strip makes one cigar.

ASSEMBLY: Place one camembert stick at the narrow end of a filo strip, 2 cm from the edge. Add 8 g pecan-cranberry mix alongside the cheese. Fold the two long sides inward over the filling (like a spring roll), then roll tightly from the cheese end toward the open end. Seal the final edge with a small brush of melted butter. Place seam-side down on baking paper-lined tray.
FREEZE: Open-freeze cigars on tray uncovered for 2 hours until fully solid. Vacuum seal in bags of 25. Label with name, date, yield. Freeze for up to 4 weeks.
DEFROST: Transfer from freezer to coldroom 24 hours before service. Do not defrost at room temperature — filo will go soggy from condensation.

THYME HONEY: Combine 300 g honey, 2 cinnamon sticks and stripped thyme leaves in a small saucepan. Warm over the lowest burner setting — honey should reach 60–65°C maximum. Do not boil — boiling destroys the volatile aromatic compounds in thyme and caramelises the honey. Hold at 60°C for 10 minutes, then remove from heat. Strain through a fine sieve. Cool, transfer to squeeze bottle or PC. Label. Store at room temperature — honey crystallises in the fridge.
service: Bring fryers to 175°C. Do not fry at higher temperature — filo browns faster than the cheese melts, resulting in a cold centre and burst seam. Fry cigars in batches of 8 maximum for 2.5–3 minutes, turning gently at the 90-second mark for even colour. Target colour: deep amber gold. Remove to wire rack.
Service window: 5 minutes maximum from fryer. Camembert sets again as it cools and loses the molten interior that defines this canape.
PLATE: Place one cigar in each bamboo boat. Drizzle 5 ml thyme honey directly over cigar using a squeeze bottle — do not pool honey in the boat base. Finish with a small pinch of microherbs on top.
Arrange 10 boats per platter. Garnish platter with a light scatter of remaining pecan crumb and a few whole cranberries for colour cues. Send immediately.
note: Garlic removed entirely — it was in the original spec but conflicts with the thyme-honey profile and the delicate lactic flavour of camembert. Butter was listed in method steps but absent from the original ingredient list — added at 150 g, calculated at 3 g per double-buttered strip with buffer. Filo packet quantities increased from 2 to 3 — 2 packets do not yield sufficient strips for 50 cigars when accounting for tears and trim waste. Fry temperature reduced to 175°C from implied high heat to prevent blowout.

name: Romesco
subtitle: House Sauce
label: Romesco
method_steps: Heat 30 ml olive oil in a wide heavy-based pot over medium heat. Sauté onion and garlic, stirring regularly, for 10–12 minutes until fully softened and lightly golden.
Transfer sautéed onion and garlic to a blender. Add blitzed capsicum, passata, basil, sugar, salt and pepper. Blend on high until completely smooth.

Return blended mixture to the pot. Bring to a gentle simmer over medium-low heat, then reduce heat to low. Simmer uncovered for 90 minutes, stirring every 15–20 minutes, until sauce is thick, rich and reduced by roughly one-quarter.
Stir in thickened cream. Simmer a further 10 minutes. Taste and adjust salt, pepper and sugar.
Cool to below 21°C before portioning. Transfer to labelled PCs, date and freeze.
service: Reheat from frozen in a saucepan over medium heat, stirring, until reaching 75°C minimum. Alternatively reheat in microwave in 60-second bursts, stirring between. Use as sauce base or accompaniment as required.
note: Made in-house using house tomato sugo. Freezes well — portion into PCs, label and freeze. Defrost overnight in coldroom. Also the base for Vodka Sauce.

name: Lemon Dill Aioli
subtitle: House component
label: Lemon Dill Aioli

ingredients.prep: zest finely microplaned, juiced — approx. 90 ml juice
fronds only, finely chopped — approx. 20 g picked

method_steps: Pick dill fronds and finely chop. Set aside.
Microplane lemon zest directly into a large bowl to capture oils. Juice lemons, strain seeds.
Add Kewpie mayonnaise, chopped dill, lemon juice, salt and white pepper to the bowl with the zest. Mix thoroughly until uniform.
Taste and adjust — add more lemon juice for brightness or salt as needed.
Transfer to a labelled PC, press cling film to surface and refrigerate. Use within 4 days.
service: Serve chilled alongside Calamari Fritti (tapas and canapé formats). Portion into ramekins or squeeze bottle for service. Stir before portioning if held overnight.
note: Pepper updated to white pepper for cleaner colour in the finished aioli. Adjust lemon quantity to season — acid level of lemons varies.

name: Lemon Thyme Aioli
subtitle: House component
label: Lemon Thyme Aioli

ingredients.prep: zest finely microplaned, juiced — approx. 90 ml juice
leaves stripped from stems — approx. 15 g picked

method_steps: Strip thyme leaves from stems. Discard woody stems.
Microplane lemon zest into a large bowl. Juice lemons, strain seeds.
Add Kewpie mayonnaise, thyme leaves, lemon juice, salt and white pepper to the zest bowl. Mix thoroughly until uniform.
Taste and adjust seasoning. Transfer to a labelled PC, press cling film to surface and refrigerate. Use within 4 days.
service: Serve chilled alongside arancini, chorizo potatoes or as required. Portion into ramekins or squeeze bottle for service. Stir before portioning if held overnight.
note: Thyme leaves are more assertive than dill — taste carefully before finalising acid and salt balance. Avoid over-picking; leaves should be small and tender.

name: Vodka Sauce
subtitle: Uses Romesco
label: Vodka Sauce
method_steps: Heat olive oil in a wide heavy-based pot over medium heat. Sauté garlic and chilli flakes for 2–3 minutes until fragrant, stirring constantly — do not colour.
Add tomato paste and cook out for 3–4 minutes, stirring, until it deepens to a brick-red colour.
Carefully add vodka and allow to reduce by half over medium heat, approximately 3–4 minutes.
Add warmed romesco and sugar. Stir to combine and bring to a gentle simmer.
Simmer uncovered over low heat for 25–30 minutes, stirring regularly, until sauce is thick and rich.
Stir in thickened cream. Simmer a further 10 minutes. Taste and adjust salt, pepper and sugar.
Cool to below 21°C before portioning. Transfer to labelled PCs, date and refrigerate or freeze.
service: Reheat from cold in a saucepan over medium heat to 75°C minimum, stirring regularly. Thin with a small amount of pasta water or cream if needed. Use as a pasta sauce base or accompaniment.
note: Built on house romesco which uses in-house sugo. Sugar quantity approx. 50 g — taste and adjust as romesco batch acidity varies.

name: Whipped Butter
subtitle: Bread service
label: Whipped Butter

ingredients.prep: cut into cubes, softened to room temperature (approx. 20°C)
plus extra to finish — Murray River pink salt or Maldon recommended

ingredients.item: Optional flavour additions — choose one per batch:

method_steps: Cube butter and allow to soften fully at room temperature until pliable — approximately 30–45 minutes depending on ambient temperature. Do not melt.
Place softened butter in the bowl of a stand mixer fitted with the whisk attachment. Whip on medium speed for 3 minutes, then increase to high speed.
Whip on high for a further 5–8 minutes until the butter is very pale, light and fluffy. Scrape down the bowl halfway through.
Add salt flakes and any optional flavour addition. Whip a further 60 seconds to incorporate evenly.
Taste and adjust salt.
Transfer to a piping bag fitted with a large open star nozzle (1.5–2 cm). Pipe into small service dishes or ramekins in generous rosettes. Finish with a pinch of flake salt on top.
Refrigerate immediately on a flat tray. Cover with cling film once set. Hold refrigerated at 2–4°C.
service: Remove from refrigerator 10–15 minutes before service so butter softens slightly and is spreadable.
Serve with house bread. Present one ramekin per table at bread drop.
Do not re-pipe returned butter. Discard after service.
note: Flavour variants: plain salt flake is the house default. Rosemary-lemon and roasted garlic are suitable seasonal alternatives — confirm with head chef before switching. Hold time: piped and refrigerated, use within 3 days. Do not freeze piped portions — texture deteriorates. Bulk unwhipped butter can be frozen prior to whipping.

name: Riviera House Emulsion
subtitle: House Sauce
label: Emulsion

ingredients.prep: roots trimmed and discarded, leaves and stems used
zest finely microplaned, juiced — approx. 400–450 ml juice
stems removed; seeds retained for heat or removed for mild

method_steps: Microplane lemon zest directly into the blender jug to capture oils. Juice lemons and add juice to the jug.
Trim coriander roots and discard. Roughly chop all herbs — no need for precision as they will be blended.
Add apricots, all herbs, red wine vinegar, olive oil, jalapeños, garlic, caster sugar, salt and pepper to the blender.
Blend on high speed for 60–90 seconds until completely smooth. If using a bar blender, blend in two batches.
Taste carefully and adjust: add salt for seasoning, sugar to balance acidity, extra vinegar for brightness, or extra jalapeño for heat.
Transfer to a labelled PC and refrigerate. For bulk storage or freezing: pour a thin layer of olive oil (approx. 20 ml) over the surface before sealing to prevent oxidation and freezer burn.
service: Serve cold or at room temperature. Drizzle generously over protein — chicken skewers, grilled lamb, seafood platters. Stir or shake well before service as the emulsion may separate on standing.
note: Olive oil cap before freezing is essential to prevent surface oxidation — do not skip. The oil layer stirs back through on defrosting. Defrost overnight in the coldroom. Jalapeño heat varies significantly by season — taste and adjust seed inclusion accordingly.

name: Labneh Tzatziki
subtitle: Pickled Cucumber · Pickled Cucumber Brine · Lemon
label: Labneh Tzatziki
method_steps: Finely dice pickled cucumber. Very finely dice red and brown onion. Microplane garlic.
In a large bowl, combine labneh, diced pickled cucumber, pickled cucumber brine, red onion, brown onion, grated garlic and chopped dill.
Add olive oil, lemon juice and lemon zest. Season with salt and white pepper.
Mix until completely uniform. Taste and adjust — add more brine for tang, lemon for brightness, or salt as needed.
Transfer to a labelled PC, press cling film to surface and refrigerate. Hold at 2–4°C. Use within 3 days.
service: Serve chilled. Use for chicken skewers, lamb dishes, mezze platters or canapé garnish.
Stir well before each service. Portion into ramekins or pipe onto plates as required.
note: House version uses pickled cucumber (not raw) — diced pickled cucumber + brine gives brightness and crunch without raw cucumber. Scale: 1 kg labneh batch. Scaled from the 3 kg house batch.

name: Pickled Cucumber Brine
subtitle: Apple Cider Vinegar · Seeded Mustard · Dill
label: Cucumber Brine
method_steps: Combine water, apple cider vinegar, seeded mustard, white pepper, table salt, sugar, MSG and bay leaves in a medium saucepan.
Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat, stirring to fully dissolve salt and sugar — approximately 3–4 minutes. Do not boil hard.
Remove from heat immediately once dissolved. Add whole dill sprigs.
Allow to cool to room temperature, then refrigerate until completely cold (below 10°C) before using — do not pour hot brine over cucumbers.
Slice cucumbers as required and pack into clean sealed containers. Pour cold brine over to fully submerge. Seal, label and refrigerate.
Cucumbers are ready after a minimum 24 hours. Flavour develops further over 48–72 hours.
service: Use pickled cucumbers in Labneh Tzatziki and as garnish applications. Reserve brine — it is an active ingredient in the tzatziki recipe and can be used to adjust acidity in dressings and sauces.
note: House batch targets approximately 6 cucumbers. Sugar quantity converted to metric (20 g) for consistency. Brine must be fully cold before use — hot brine softens cucumbers and reduces crunch. Brine can be strained and reused once for a second batch if cucumbers are added within 5 days; discard after second use.

name: Sweet Thyme Chicken Marinade
subtitle: Honey · Limoncello · Lemon · Garlic
label: Chicken Marinade

ingredients.prep: stripped from stems — approx. 1.5 tsp picked

method_steps: Combine honey, limoncello, olive oil, lemon zest and lemon juice in a bowl. Whisk until honey is fully dissolved and mixture is emulsified.
Add garlic paste, thyme leaves, salt and cracked pepper. Whisk again until uniform.
Coat chicken pieces thoroughly, ensuring all surfaces are covered. For skewers: toss cut pieces in marinade directly in a container.
Cover or seal, label with date and protein, and refrigerate. Marinate for a minimum of 6 hours, ideally 12 hours, before cooking.
service: Best applied to skewers, grilled thigh fillets or breast pieces.
For breast: marinate 6–8 hours maximum to avoid acid-toughening from lemon. For thigh: can hold up to 24 hours chilled.
Remove from coldroom and allow to temper for 15–20 minutes before cooking for even heat penetration.
Cook skewers on chargrill or in kick oven at 200°C until internal temperature reaches 74°C minimum.
note: House ratio: 1 nip (30 ml) limoncello per 1 kg chicken — scale proportionally for larger batches. Thyme quantity converted from volume to weight (5 g) for batch scaling accuracy.

name: Lamb Citrus Herb Brine
subtitle: Rosemary · Thyme · Garlic · Lemon
label: Lamb Brine
method_steps: Combine salt, sugar and 1 L hot water in a large container or brine pot. Stir until salt and sugar are fully dissolved.
Add crushed garlic, rosemary, thyme and sliced lemons. Stir to combine.
Add 4 L cold water. Stir thoroughly and check temperature — brine must be below 5°C before adding lamb. Refrigerate or add ice if needed to reach temperature.
Submerge lamb completely in brine. Weigh down with a plate or lid to keep submerged if needed.
Cover, label with date and time, and refrigerate in the coldroom. Brine for exactly 24 hours.
service: After 24 hours, drain lamb and discard brine. Do not reuse.
Pat lamb completely dry with paper towel. Allow to temper at room temperature for 20–30 minutes before cooking.

Grill on chargrill over high heat or cook in kick oven as required by the specific recipe. For Chargrilled Lamb Cutlets: sear on chargrill at high heat 2–3 minutes per side to achieve char, then rest 3–4 minutes tented loosely before service. Internal target: 60–62°C for medium-rare.
note: This brine is the standard 24-hour pre-cook brine for the Chargrilled Lamb Cutlet recipe. Timing is critical — over-brining beyond 24 hours will result in overly salty, texturally compromised meat. Always brine in the coldroom, never at ambient temperature. Batch is scaled for 5 kg lamb — scale salt and sugar proportionally for smaller batches (36 g salt and 24 g sugar per 1 kg lamb).

name: Date Madeleines
subtitle: Date & Brown Sugar Batter · 160°C Conventional · 14 Minutes
yield: 1 kg date batch — approx. 40–45 madeleines depending on tray size
label: Date Madeleines

ingredients.prep: roughly chopped — Medjool preferred for moisture content; Deglet Noor will produce a drier result
approx. 3.5 tsp — reacts with boiling water to soften dates and aerate batter
measured by weight — 895 g — for precision
room temperature — cold eggs will split the butter-sugar emulsion
sifted with flour — absent from original, critical for flavour balance in a brown sugar batter

ingredients.qty: neutral spray oil or melted butter
ingredients.prep: for greasing trays — madeleines are notorious for sticking without proper release

method_steps: DATE SOAK: Place 1 kg chopped dates in a large heatproof bowl. Add 12 g bicarbonate of soda. Pour 895 ml boiling water over dates immediately and stir once to distribute bicarb. Rest uncovered for 20 minutes until dates are fully softened and water has cooled to below 40°C. Do not blend — the texture of softened date pieces in the finished madeleine is intentional.

TRAY PREP: Grease madeleine trays thoroughly with spray oil or brushed melted butter. Dust lightly with plain flour, tap out excess. Alternatively, use a silicone madeleine mat — no greasing required. Refrigerate greased trays while making batter — a cold tray improves the formation of the characteristic madeleine hump.
CREAM: In a stand mixer fitted with the paddle attachment, cream 145 g brown sugar and 285 g softened butter on medium speed for 4 minutes until pale and fluffy. Scrape bowl sides at 2 minutes.

EGGS: Add 7 room-temperature eggs one at a time with mixer on medium-low, allowing each egg to fully incorporate before adding the next — approx. 30 seconds per egg. If the mixture splits or looks curdled, add 1 tbsp of the sifted flour and continue — the flour will re-emulsify the mix.

DRY INGREDIENTS: Sift together 660 g plain flour, 22 g baking powder and 4 g fine salt. With mixer on low, add dry ingredients in three additions, mixing until just combined after each. Do not overmix — overworking the flour develops gluten and produces tough madeleines.
DATE FOLD: Add the cooled date mixture (including all soaking liquid) to the batter. Fold through with a spatula until just combined. Batter will be loose and pourable — this is correct.
REST (recommended): Rest batter in fridge for 30 minutes minimum before piping. Cold batter produces a better rise and more defined shell pattern.

BAKE: Pipe or spoon batter into prepared madeleine trays, filling each cell to 80% capacity. Bake in conventional oven (not fan-forced) at 160°C for 14 minutes. Do not open oven in the first 10 minutes. Madeleines are done when the hump has risen and set and the edges are pulling away from the tray slightly. A skewer inserted in the centre should come out clean.
COOL: Remove from oven. Rest in tray 3 minutes, then turn out onto a wire cooling rack immediately. Do not leave in trays — steam trapped beneath the madeleine will soften the shell base.
service: Cool fully on wire racks before storing — minimum 20 minutes at room temperature.
Store in an airtight container, single layers separated by baking paper. Madeleines are best consumed within 24 hours of baking — the shell softens noticeably after 48 hours.
For service: arrange on a lined platter or slate. Optional: dust lightly with icing sugar immediately before serving.
note: Scaled from the 280 g date house formula — quantities rounded to kitchen-practical measurements. Salt added to dry mix (4 g) — absent from original, essential for lifting the brown sugar and date sweetness. Tsp measurements for leavening agents converted to grams for batch consistency. Conventional oven specified — fan-forced creates uneven surface drying before the hump has time to form. Cold tray technique added — standard for madeleine production.

name: Kilpatrick Sauce
subtitle: BBQ · Worcestershire · Smoked Paprika · Brown Sugar
label: Kilpatrick Sauce

ingredients.prep: approx. 1–1.5 tsp — adjust to heat preference

method_steps: Combine BBQ sauce, Worcestershire sauce, brown sugar, smoked paprika and chilli flakes in a small saucepan.
Place over medium-low heat. Whisk to combine and dissolve sugar. Bring to a gentle simmer.
Simmer for 5–8 minutes, whisking occasionally, until sauce is glossy, slightly thickened and coats the back of a spoon.
Remove from heat. Taste and adjust chilli flakes for heat or sugar for balance.
Cool to room temperature before decanting into a squeeze bottle or labelled storage container. Refrigerate. Use within 7 days.
service: Spoon or pipe directly onto freshly shucked oysters before finishing under the salamander at 220°C for 3–4 minutes until bubbling and caramelised.
Can also be used as a smoky-sweet glaze brushed onto proteins during the final stages of grilling.
note: Set to mild-medium heat profile. Increase chilli flakes to 5 g for a hotter version if required. Brown sugar and spice quantities converted to metric weights for batch scaling accuracy.

name: Calamari Flour Mix
subtitle: Rice Flour · Corn Flour · Lemon Pepper
label: Calamari Flour Mix
method_steps: Weigh all ingredients by chosen part size (e.g. 200 g per part for a medium batch) into a large dry bowl.
Whisk thoroughly until colour and seasoning are completely uniform throughout — no clumps or streaks of paprika.
Transfer to an airtight labelled container. Store in a cool, dry area away from moisture. Label with batch date.

Before service: set up a shallow tray or bowl with the flour mix at the fry station. Toss calamari rings in the mix immediately before frying — do not pre-coat and hold, as moisture will clump the coating.
Fry at 180°C in the fryer for 2–2.5 minutes until golden and crisp. Shake excess flour before dropping into oil.
service: Shake excess flour from calamari before dropping into fryer to prevent oil contamination.
Fry in small batches to maintain oil temperature. Drain on a wire rack, season lightly and serve immediately.
note: House ratio recipe — scale up or down by part size. A standard prep batch is typically 200 g per part (total 650 g). Confirm fryer oil temperature with a probe before service — target 180°C.

name: House Chip Seasoning
subtitle: Chicken Salt · Lemon Pepper · Smoked Paprika · MSG
label: Chip Seasoning
method_steps: Weigh each ingredient individually into a large dry bowl.
Whisk vigorously until colour is completely uniform — no visible streaks of paprika or cumin. Total batch weight: 2.1 kg.
Transfer to an airtight labelled dry-store container. Store away from moisture and heat. Label with batch date.
Season chips immediately after frying while still hot — seasoning adheres best when oil is fresh on the surface.
service: Apply seasoning to chips immediately out of the fryer while draining on the rack.
Toss or shake chips with seasoning in a metal bowl for even coverage. Do not over-season — the mix is high in salt.
note: House ratio batch: 600 g chicken salt, 50 g MSG, 100 g cracked black pepper, 50 g smoked paprika, 50 g cumin, 70 g garlic powder, 30 g oregano, 150 g lemon pepper, 900 g table salt, 100 g sugar. Scale proportionally as needed. Chicken salt already contains salt — the addition of 900 g table salt makes this a high-sodium blend; taste before applying heavily and ensure portion discipline at the fry station.

name: Heritage Chip Seasoning
subtitle: House Blend · Salt · Herbs · Spice
label: Chip Seasoning

ingredients.prep: adjust for audience — omit for children's menus

method_steps: BLEND: Weigh all ingredients precisely. Combine in a large bowl and whisk thoroughly until fully uniform — no visible streaking of individual spices.
SIEVE: Pass the entire blend through a fine-mesh sieve to break up any clumps, particularly from the garlic and onion powders. Discard any large particles that won't pass through.
STORE: Transfer to a labelled airtight container. Store away from heat, light, and humidity. Date and initial the container.
service: Apply to hot chips immediately out of the fryer while oil is still on the surface — seasoning adheres best to hot, moist chips. Season in a bowl, not on a flat tray, and toss to coat evenly.
Standard application: 3–4g per 150g portion of chips. Adjust to taste.
note: House blend — batch weekly or as required. Total yield approximately 158g without cayenne, 160g with. Adjust cayenne to event audience — reduce to 1g or omit entirely for children's menus. Store in a labelled airtight container; keep away from humidity as garlic and onion powders cake quickly. Check and re-sieve if stored longer than 7 days.

name: Reef Fish Batter
subtitle: Sparkling Water · Seasoned Flour · Vodka
label: Reef Fish Batter

ingredients.prep: very cold — keep refrigerated until use

method_steps: Combine self-raising flour, salt flakes, cracked pepper, garlic powder and onion powder in a large cold bowl. Whisk dry ingredients to distribute evenly.

Gradually pour in cold sparkling water while whisking — add three-quarters first and whisk to a smooth pancake-batter consistency, then add remaining water to achieve a batter that coats the back of a spoon and drips off cleanly.
Add vodka and stir briefly until just incorporated. Do not over-mix — small lumps are acceptable and help with texture.
Keep batter bowl over ice or in the coldroom. Use immediately or within 30 minutes for best lift and crunch.
Dip reef fish pieces fully in batter, allow excess to drip off, then lower carefully into fryer oil at 180°C. Fry for 3–4 minutes until deeply golden and cooked through.
service: Whisk batter lightly between batches — do not over-aerate.
Keep batter cold throughout service. Replace with a fresh batch if batter becomes warm, thick or flat.
Drain fried fish on a wire rack, not paper towel, to maintain crunch. Season lightly with salt flakes immediately after frying.
note: Extracted from the Crispy Reef Fish Slider recipe as a standalone prep card. Vodka reduces gluten development and promotes a crisper, lighter batter. Cold sparkling water is essential — warm or flat water will result in a dense, doughy coating. Confirm fryer oil temperature at 180°C with a probe before service.

name: Mild Hot Honey
subtitle: Thyme · Chilli Flakes · Lemon
label: Hot Honey

ingredients.prep: approx. 6 tsp — adjust to heat preference
zest finely microplaned — no white pith
freshly squeezed, approx. 2–3 lemons — add to taste after infusion

method_steps: Combine honey, chilli flakes, thyme sprigs and lemon zest in a heavy-based saucepan.

Warm over low heat, stirring gently, until honey reaches approximately 60–65°C. Maintain this temperature for 10 minutes to infuse — do not allow to simmer or boil, as this will destroy the aromatic compounds and darken the honey.
Remove from heat. Rest uncovered for 15–20 minutes to continue infusing as it cools.
Taste for heat level and herb character. Strain through a fine sieve to remove thyme sprigs and chilli flakes if a smooth finish is preferred, or leave unstrained for a rustic pour.
Once cooled to below 40°C, stir in lemon juice to taste — start with 60 ml and adjust.
Cool fully to room temperature. Transfer to clean sealed squeeze bottles or jars. Label with batch date.
service: Use as a mild sweet-heat drizzle over proteins, canapés and platters.
Serve at room temperature — honey thickens when cold. If too thick for service, warm the bottle briefly in warm water.
Apply as a finishing drizzle, not a cooking glaze — heat will destroy the lemon and thyme aromatics.
note: Mild heat profile by design. For a medium-heat version, increase chilli flakes to 10 g. The 60–65°C infusion temperature is important — use a probe thermometer to confirm. Honey held at stable room temperature in sealed containers keeps well for up to 4 weeks. Refrigeration is not required but will extend shelf life — warm before service if refrigerated.

name: Charcuterie Board
subtitle: Brie · Blue · Cheddar · Prosciutto · Salami · Crackers
yield: 1 board — serves 10–12 guests as a standalone or 15–20 as part of a wider spread
label: Charcuterie Board

ingredients.prep: thin sliced, folded into quarters or roses
tempered to 16–18°C, cut into wedges with rind intact
tempered to 16–18°C, broken into irregular pieces
tempered to 16–18°C, sliced or portioned into wedges
60 g each e.g. water crackers + seeded crispbread
washed, cut into small clusters of 4–6 berries
e.g. quince paste, romesco, caramelised onion, or hot honey — served in a small ramekin
whole, arranged upright or flat for height and colour
dates and/or dried apricots, halved or whole

method_steps: TEMPER CHEESES: Remove all three cheeses from coldroom 30–40 minutes before assembly. Cheese served below 14°C loses aroma and texture — this is non-negotiable.
BOARD PREP: Select a board minimum 45 cm × 35 cm. Place a small ramekin for the chef's dip first — this anchors the layout.
CHEESE FIRST: Position the three cheeses at different zones across the board — brie centre, blue at one end, cheddar at the other. Space them so guests can access each without disturbing the others.
MEATS: Fan salami roses adjacent to the blue cheese. Arrange torn prosciutto ribbons loosely near the brie. Fold and layer — do not lay flat.
CRACKERS: Fan two varieties along opposite board edges, away from wet elements (grapes, dip) to prevent sogging.

FRUIT & GARNISH: Scatter grape clusters, dates and apricots into the gaps between cheese and meat zones. Stand dried orange and lemon slices upright against cheese pieces for height and colour contrast.
DIP: Fill ramekin with chef's dip of choice. Position centrally or near the cheddar.
HOLD: If assembling more than 20 minutes before service, cover loosely with cling film and return to coldroom. Remove 10 minutes before service.
service: Place board at room temperature — cheeses must be at 16–18°C at service for correct flavour and texture.
Replenish crackers every 10 minutes during passed or display service — they go first.
Replace picked-at cheese pieces if board is on display longer than 30 minutes.
note: Chef's dip rotates by season or event — quince paste pairs with blue, hot honey with brie, romesco with cheddar and meats. Dried citrus slices are a house garnish — source pre-dried or dehydrate in-house at 80°C for 3–4 hours. Crackers must be kept away from grapes and dip ramekin to stay crisp.

name: Nibble Platter
subtitle: 40 Pieces · 10/10/10/10 Split
label: Nibble Platter

ingredients.prep: cut to canape size — approx. 5–6 cm lengths if full-size rolls used
whole or halved depending on size
individual cocktail size — confirm with supplier that pies are 60–80 g each
individual cocktail size or pre-portioned wedges — confirm portion size
served in a small ramekin on the side — standard accompaniment expectation
served in a small ramekin — for sausage rolls and pies

method_steps: REHEAT PROTOCOL: Preheat kick oven to 190°C (fan-forced). Place all pastry items on wire rack trays — not solid baking paper, which traps steam and softens pastry bases. Reheat times: sausage rolls 10–12 minutes, beef pies 12–14 minutes, quiche Lorraine 10 minutes, spinach pizzinis 8–10 minutes. All items must reach a minimum internal temperature of 75°C before service.

STAGGER THE BAKE: With a 2-person team and one kick oven, bake in two rounds. Round 1: pies and sausage rolls (longest reheat). Round 2: quiche and pizzinis. This prevents a cold-hold gap where early items drop below 60°C while waiting for the rest.

HOLD HOT: If not serving immediately, hold in a bain-marie or covered tray at 65°C minimum. Do not hold on open trays at room temperature — pastry softens within 10 minutes and pork/beef filling enters the temperature danger zone.

ROOM TEMP SERVICE OPTION: If serving at room temperature (standing cocktail style), bake to order in smaller batches. Pull from oven at 75°C internal temp, rest 5 minutes uncovered on wire rack, then plate. Surface will crisp briefly on resting. Service window is 15 minutes at room temp before pastry becomes unacceptably soft.
service: Line platter with white baking paper or a paper doily — these items release grease and a bare platter looks poor within 5 minutes.

Arrange in a 10/10/10/10 split with each variety in its own clearly defined zone. Label each zone with a small card if serving to a large group or at a dietary-conscious event — quiche Lorraine contains egg and dairy, sausage rolls and pies contain gluten and meat.
Place one ramekin of tomato sauce and one of mustard at platter corners. Replenish platter from a warm hold tray — never add cold items to a platter that has been out for more than 20 minutes.
Send immediately from oven. Pastry canapes have a hard service window — communicate this clearly in the run sheet.
note: Reheat temperatures and internal temperature targets added — original spec had no food safety controls for reheated meat pastry products, which is a critical gap. Staggered bake protocol added for 2-person team with one kick oven. Condiment ramekins added — standard guest expectation for this style of platter and a genuine omission in the original. Wire rack baking specified to prevent steam-softened bases.

name: Bruschetta of Rare Roast Beef
subtitle: Charred Sourdough · Whipped Horseradish Cream · Pickled Shallot · Chive
label: Beef Bruschetta

ingredients.prep: slice 1.5cm thick, brush both sides with olive oil, char on grill or cast iron pan until deep grill marks both sides — not pale
trimmed of sinew, brought to room temperature 30 min before searing
cold from fridge — must be cold to whip
drained thoroughly on paper towel, excess moisture pressed out
split — 15ml for pickle, 5ml for horseradish cream
for searing beef — high smoke point
generous seasoning on beef before sear

method_steps: PICKLE: Combine 15ml red wine vinegar, 3g salt flakes, 5g caster sugar in a small bowl; whisk to dissolve. Add shallot rings, press to submerge, rest minimum 30 min at room temperature. Drain on paper towel before service. Do not rinse — the residual acidity is intentional.

HORSERADISH CREAM: Whip cold thickened cream in a chilled bowl to soft-to-medium peaks — do not over-whip to stiff (texture becomes grainy once folded). Fold in drained horseradish and 5ml red wine vinegar gently; you want ribbons of horseradish visible, not a homogeneous paste. Season with salt flakes and white pepper. Transfer to piping bag or squeeze bottle; hold chilled in PC at 2–4°C. Use same day.

BEEF: Pat striploin dry. Season aggressively on all faces with salt flakes and cracked pepper at least 10 min before searing (ideally 30 min). Heat a heavy-based pan or cast iron over high heat until smoking; add neutral oil. Sear striploin 2–3 min per face until deep mahogany crust forms (Maillard — do not move meat). Sear short ends 30 sec each. Internal temp target: 45–48°C core for rare (residual carry-over will reach 50–52°C). Rest on a wire rack, uncovered, minimum 10 min. Do NOT wrap — trapped steam softens the crust. Slice paper-thin (2–3mm) across the grain at service; use a sharp slicer or carving knife. Hold sliced beef lightly covered at room temp; service same day within 2 hours of slicing.

CROSTINI: Brush sourdough slices both sides with olive oil. Char on a ribbed grill pan or solid grill over high heat until firm grill marks appear and bread is crisp through — approximately 2 min per side. Cool on wire rack; do not stack (steam = soggy base). Crostini can be prepared up to 2 hours before service; hold uncovered at room temp.

ASSEMBLY: Spread or pipe a 10g smear of horseradish cream on each crostini, leaving a 3mm border. Drape 1–2 slices of beef (approx 20g) loosely — do not press flat. Top with 3–4 shallot rings. Scatter chives. Finish with a single pinch of salt flakes at the pass.
service: Room-temperature canapé: plate on slate or timber board. Finish with flaky salt at the pass — do not season earlier or moisture migrates.
Assembled hold time maximum 15 minutes before pass. Beyond that the crostini base absorbs moisture from cream and beef — bread goes limp and the contrast is lost.
If holding for a staggered pass, keep crostini, beef, and cream separate; assemble in 20-piece batches.
note: Scale beef to 1.1kg raw for a 40-piece run with buffer. Horseradish cream failure mode: over-whipping before folding causes weeping at service — soft peak only. Pickle brine sugar is essential; without it the shallot reads harsh against the cream. If striploin is well-marbled, increase cream vinegar to 8ml.

name: Spanner Crab Cannoli
subtitle: Spanner Crab · Lemon · Dill · Crème Fraîche · Crisp Shell
label: Crab Cannoli

ingredients.prep: freshly picked or premium chilled; drain in colander 30 min then press gently in clean cloth — excess moisture is the primary filling failure
full fat, softened to room temperature — do not use reduced fat
replaces 50g cream cheese; adds brightness and tang that plain cream cheese lacks
fresh squeezed, strained — reduce from 80ml; 80ml was over-acidulating and masking crab sweetness
microplane zest of 1 large lemon — aromatic lift without additional liquid
fronds only, fine chop — stalks discarded
or to heat preference; add last, taste before committing
GF if required; inspect each shell for cracks before filling — cracked shells shatter at pass
or dill fronds — garnish only, applied at pass
white pepper preferred — black specks read as shell fragments in crab

method_steps: DRAIN CRAB: Spread picked crab on a clean chux or muslin cloth set over a tray. Refrigerate uncovered 30 min to allow residual moisture to drip. Then gather cloth and press gently — do not shred the meat. Well-drained crab is non-negotiable: wet crab dilutes the cream cheese binder and causes shells to soften within 5 minutes of filling.

FILLING: Beat softened cream cheese and crème fraîche together until completely smooth — no lumps. Fold in drained crab meat with a spatula, preserving some texture (do not over-mix to a paste). Add lemon juice gradually, tasting after each addition — 30–40ml is the likely range depending on crab sweetness. Add lemon zest, dill, and Tabasco. Season with white pepper and salt flakes. The filling should be firm enough to pipe cleanly and hold shape inside the shell — if it’s loose, rest in fridge 20 min to firm. Transfer to piping bag with 1cm plain nozzle. Hold chilled in PC. Fill to order or maximum 10 min before service.

SHELLS: If using raw pastry shells, fry at 180°C in clean oil until golden and blistered — approximately 2–3 min. Drain on paper towel, season immediately with a pinch of fine salt while hot. Cool completely on a wire rack before filling (minimum 15 min — a warm shell will melt the filling and collapse).
PIPE: Fill shells from both ends to the centre 5–10 min before service. Do not fill more than 30 min out. Shells filled ahead must be kept in a single layer on a chilled tray — never stacked.
service: Pass on chilled trays or slate. Garnish each cannoli with a single dill frond or 3–4 microherb leaves applied at the pass — not pre-garnished.
A tiny pinch of lemon zest grated over the exposed filling end at the pass adds aroma on approach.
Do not offer a lemon cheek alongside — the filling is already acid-balanced.
note: Lemon juice reduced from 80ml to 40ml — 80ml was cooking the crab. Crème fraîche replaces some cream cheese: lowers pH, firms the filling, adds brightness. Fill window is 10 min maximum — communicate this at briefing. Filling ratio: 60% crab, 40% binder.

name: Prawn Brioche Slider
subtitle: Prawn Cocktail · Toasted Brioche · Pickled Slaw · Marie Rose
label: Prawn Slider

ingredients.prep: split, toasted cut-side on flat grill or pan — butter the cut face lightly before toasting for flavour and moisture barrier
peeled, tails off, halved lengthways — pat dry before folding into sauce; wet prawns dilute and break the emulsion
adds umami depth missing in original — classic Marie Rose component
quarter, core removed, shred on mandoline or box grater to 1–2mm julienne

method_steps: SLAW: Toss shredded cabbage with 5g salt; rest 20 min — salt draws excess water. Rinse briefly in cold water; squeeze dry in a clean cloth. Combine white wine vinegar and caster sugar; toss through drained cabbage. Taste — slaw should be tart, lightly sweet, and crunchy. Rest a further 10 min. Drain again before assembly. Hold chilled.

MARIE ROSE: Whisk mayonnaise, tomato sauce, lemon juice, Worcestershire, and Tabasco together until uniform. Season with white pepper and a small pinch of salt. The sauce should be pink-coral, not orange (too much tomato) and not white (too little). Adjust balance to taste. Hold chilled.
PRAWNS: Pat prawn halves dry with paper towel. Do not fold into sauce more than 30 min before service or the protein releases moisture and sauce splits. Fold gently.
BUNS: Toast cut-side of brioche on a lightly buttered flat grill or pan over medium heat until golden and caramelised — approx 60–90 sec. The butter creates a partial moisture barrier.
ASSEMBLY: Smear a small amount of Marie Rose on the base bun. Add a small mound of slaw (approx 15g, well-drained). Top with 3–4 prawn halves in cocktail sauce. Place top bun; press gently.
service: Pass within 12 minutes of assembly — brioche absorbs moisture quickly once slaw and sauce are in contact.
Slaw must have residual bite at service; if cabbage has gone limp, discard and reshred.
Skewer with a cocktail pick to hold structure during pass.
note: Worcestershire added to Marie Rose — essential for umami spine, this is not ketchup mayo. Salt the cabbage and squeeze dry before dressing — osmosis removes free water that would otherwise pool in the bun. Wet prawns in wet sauce in a wet bun is a structural failure. Keep everything as dry as possible before assembly.

name: Smoked Chicken & Bacon Brioche Slider
subtitle: Smoked Chicken · Crispy Streaky Bacon · Dijonnaise · Baby Cos
label: Chicken Slider

ingredients.prep: split, toasted cut-side on lightly buttered flat grill — butter provides moisture barrier and flavour
supplier-smoked; pull into strips or slice thin across grain — pulled reads more generously in the bun
swap from short-cut to streaky — higher fat ratio renders to a proper crisp; halved crossways after cooking
adds brightness to dijonnaise alongside lemon
inner leaves only; pat dry — do not use wet leaves in assembly

method_steps: DIJONNAISE: Whisk mayonnaise, Dijon mustard, lemon juice, and white wine vinegar until completely smooth. Season with white pepper and salt flakes — taste; the mustard carries heat and salt so add carefully. Hold chilled in squeeze bottle. Can be made a day ahead.

BACON: Lay streaky bacon rashers flat on a wire rack set over a baking tray. Roast in conventional oven at 200°C for 12–15 min until uniformly crisp and rendered — oven-rendered bacon cooks flat and evenly without curling. Drain on paper towel; season with a pinch of salt flakes while hot. Halve crossways. Hold at room temperature on a clean tray — do not refrigerate (goes chewy). Use within 2 hours.
CHICKEN: Pull or slice smoked chicken just before service. Do not sauce ahead — it will go grey and wet.
BUNS: Toast cut-side on a lightly buttered flat grill or pan over medium heat 60–90 sec until golden.

ASSEMBLY (order matters): Pipe a 5g smear of dijonnaise on both cut faces of the bun. Base bun: small cos leaf (moisture shield). Add chicken (approx 20g). Lay bacon half on top of chicken. Top bun. Press lightly and skewer.
service: Assemble in batches of 20 no more than 15 minutes before pass — bacon loses crispness quickly once in contact with moisture.
Stage bacon separately and assemble final batches at the last minute if service is staggered.
Confirm bacon is audibly crisp before each batch — if it has softened, replace.
note: Short-cut bacon does not render properly in a mini-bun — the fat collar stays rubbery. Streaky only. Order 900g smoked chicken for 40-piece buffer. Oven-rendering bacon on a rack keeps it flat and avoids 6-burner chaos at canapé run. White wine vinegar in the dijonnaise alongside lemon is a one-dimensional fix — 5ml brightens the mustard and cuts the rich smoked chicken.

name: Vintage Beef Brioche Slider
subtitle: Smash Pattie · Aged Cheddar · Cornichon · Burger Sauce
label: Beef Slider

ingredients.prep: split, toasted cut-side on buttered flat grill until golden — butter barrier critical for structural integrity
80/20 fat ratio (chuck or brisket blend preferred); loosely portion 40g balls — do not compress or pack tightly before smashing
slice 3–4mm thick — thicker slice melts in a controlled pool
sliced 2mm rounds — provides acid punctuation in every bite
from cornichon jar — adds second acid note and depth to burger sauce
season pattie only after forming and immediately before cooking

method_steps: BURGER SAUCE: Whisk mayonnaise, American mustard, tomato sauce, pickle brine, and white wine vinegar until smooth. Taste — sauce should be tangy, slightly sweet, mustardy. Adjust mustard up if flat. Hold chilled in squeeze bottle.

PATTIE PREP: Loosely ball 40g portions of mince — handle as little as possible. Do not season yet. Hold chilled on parchment-lined tray. Season both faces generously with salt flakes and cracked pepper immediately before hitting the grill — pre-salting draws protein out and produces a bouncy pattie.

COOK: Heat flat grill or heavy cast iron to high (minimum 220°C). Place mince ball; immediately smash flat with a heavy spatula to 8–9mm thickness. Press hard and hold 10 sec. Cook undisturbed 90 sec until crust is deep brown and releases cleanly. Flip once only. Lay cheddar slice immediately after flip. Cook a further 60 sec. Rest 30 sec on a warm tray.
BUNS: Toast cut-side on lightly buttered flat grill 60–90 sec until golden. Do immediately before assembly.
ASSEMBLY: Squeeze burger sauce on both cut faces. Base bun: 3–4 cornichon rounds. Pattie with melted cheddar. Top bun. Skewer with pick immediately.
service: Serve immediately — do not hold assembled. Smash patties lose their crust moisture differential within 3 minutes of plating.
Cook in batches of 10 to maintain grill surface temperature. Overcrowding drops surface temp below Maillard threshold.
At 80-cover events, run grill in rolling waves of 10 — two chefs: one grilling, one assembling. Stagger starts by 3–4 min.
note: Pickle brine in the burger sauce is the flavour architecture fix — acid, depth, and a savoury fermented note that ties the cornichon on the bun. Pre-salted mince produces rubbery, dense patties — season only at the moment of cooking. Cheddar must melt completely on the pattie before it leaves the grill.

name: Mini Beefburger Canapé
subtitle: Wagyu Smash Pattie · Brioche · Black Truffle Aioli · Microgreens
label: Mini Burger

ingredients.prep: split, toasted cut-side on buttered flat grill
MS4+ minimum; loosely portioned into 40g balls — do NOT compress. Hold chilled. Season only immediately before cooking.
full fat — base for truffle aioli
add in 2 stages — half to base, half to finish; truffle oil aroma is volatile and dissipates quickly
microplane to fine paste; rest 5 min before mixing
small addition to emulsify and add complexity to truffle aioli
dress lightly with 5ml extra virgin olive oil and a pinch of salt flakes at pass — do not pre-dress

method_steps: TRUFFLE AIOLI: Whisk mayonnaise, Dijon mustard, garlic paste, lemon juice, and half the truffle oil (7.5ml) until fully combined. Season. Hold chilled in squeeze bottle. Add remaining 7.5ml truffle oil at the pass just before service to refresh aroma.

PATTIE PREP: Loosely ball 40g portions of Wagyu mince, handling minimally. Do not season until immediately before cooking. Wagyu at MS4+ begins rendering intramuscular fat at approximately 40°C — the window between medium-rare and overcooked is approximately 30 seconds. Have tray and resting surface ready before the first pattie goes on.

COOK: Heat flat grill or cast iron to high (220°C+). Place Wagyu ball; smash immediately to 8–9mm. Season top face with salt and pepper after smashing. Cook undisturbed 75 sec. Flip once; cook 45–60 sec only. Do NOT add cheese to Wagyu smash — additional dairy fat tips the richness over for a one-bite canapé. Rest 30–45 sec on warm tray.
BUNS: Toast cut-side on lightly buttered flat grill 60–90 sec until golden. Assemble immediately.

ASSEMBLY: Squeeze 5g truffle aioli on base bun. Place pattie. Top with a small cluster of dressed microgreens (4–5 leaves, no more). Top bun. Skewer with pick. Finish with a single pinch of salt flakes over the exposed pattie edge if visible.
service: Serve immediately — Wagyu fat solidifies as temperature drops and texture becomes unpleasant within 4 minutes of leaving the grill.
Cook in batches of 8–10 maximum. Surface temp recovery between batches is critical — Wagyu fat load will drop the grill temp faster than standard mince.
Two chefs, rolling batches, 3-minute intervals. One grills, one assembles and passes.
note: Truffle oil added in 2 stages: half to base integrates into the emulsion; half at pass hits the nose on approach. Without the split, the truffle note is flat by service. 75-sec/45-sec cook split is corrected for Wagyu vs standard mince. Smash technique gives consistent result without chasing core temps at 80 covers.

name: Beef Polpette
subtitle: Napoli Sauce · Parmesan Reggiano · Torn Basil · Toasted Crumb
label: Beef Polpette

ingredients.prep: 20% fat content minimum — lean mince will produce dry, bouncy polpette
do not omit — pork fat and connective tissue provides gelatin and tenderness
from day-old white loaf, blitzed coarse — not panko (too dry); soak in milk before use
for soaking breadcrumbs — panade technique; this is the single most important step for a tender polpette
finely grated on microplane; plus extra 20g to serve
leaves only, fine chop — weigh approx 20g picked leaves
warmed and tasted for seasoning before adding polpette
for searing — not extra virgin; smoke point too low
toast in dry pan with 5ml olive oil and a pinch of salt until golden — finish crumb for texture contrast at service
leaves only, torn at pass — never chop basil; oxidises immediately

method_steps: PANADE: Combine fresh breadcrumbs and milk in a small bowl; stir and rest 5 min until milk is fully absorbed and crumbs form a soft paste. This panade swells during cooking and physically separates the protein strands — the mechanism for a tender polpette. Skipping this step produces rubbery, dense balls.

MIX: Combine beef mince, pork mince, soaked panade, eggs, Parmesan, garlic paste, and parsley in a large bowl. Season assertively — cold mince mutes flavour; season 10–15% more than you think is needed. Mix until just combined — overmixing activates myosin proteins excessively and produces a tough texture. Test: fry a small piece, taste for seasoning and texture before committing to the full batch.
FORM: Portion mixture into 30g balls using a scoop or two spoons. Roll between palms briefly to shape — do not compress tightly. Chill 20 min before searing.

SEAR: Heat olive oil in a wide, heavy-based pan over high heat until shimmering. Sear polpette in batches — single layer only, space between each. Do not move for 90 sec until crust releases cleanly. Rotate to colour all sides — approximately 4 min total per batch. Crowded pan = steam = grey polpette.

BRAISE: Warm Napoli sauce to a gentle simmer (80–85°C). Add seared polpette; spoon sauce over. Cover and cook at 80–85°C for 18–20 min until cooked through (internal temp 72°C). Do not boil — aggressive simmering breaks the polpette surface. Hold in sauce in bain-marie at 75°C.
TOASTED CRUMB: Toast panko in a dry pan with 5ml olive oil and a pinch of salt over medium heat, stirring constantly, until uniformly golden — approx 3–4 min. Cool on paper towel. Apply at pass only.
service: Serve 1 polpette per spoon or Chinese-style soup spoon. Spoon a small amount of Napoli sauce into the spoon base first.
At the pass: finish with a pinch of finely grated Parmesan, a small cluster of torn basil, and a pinch of toasted crumb applied last.
Hold polpette in sauce in bain-marie at 75°C — do not exceed 80°C or the exterior begins to tighten and dry.
Toasted crumb must be applied at the final moment of plating — it absorbs moisture from the sauce within 60 seconds.
note: The panade (milk-soaked breadcrumbs) is non-negotiable — do not substitute with dry crumbs. The sear step must not be rushed — steamed grey balls going into the braise produces no fond and no texture development. Have 200ml additional warm Napoli ready at the pass to adjust sauce consistency. The toasted crumb is the textural fix — polpette on a spoon with only sauce is texturally monotonous.

name: Slow Cooked Lamb Shoulder Provençale
subtitle: Pulled Lamb Shoulder · Provençale Vegetables · Olive & Herb Jus · Gremolata
label: Lamb Provençale

ingredients.prep: bone-in, 2 shoulders approx — trimmed of excess surface fat but fat cap left on
halved horizontally, no peel needed for braise
pitted, halved — added at jus finish only
