washed, spun dry, picked to single leaves for spoon service

method_steps: CHORIZO: Heat 20 ml olive oil in a wide heavy-based pan over medium heat. Add chorizo in a single layer. Render 3–4 min, turning once, until fat is released and edges are crisp. Remove with slotted spoon to a tray lined with paper towel. Reserve pan with chorizo fat — this is your flavour base.

PRAWN PREP: Prawns must be completely dry before they hit the pan. Any surface moisture causes steaming, not searing — the Maillard reaction cannot occur above 100°C if water is present. Pat each prawn on fresh paper towel immediately before cooking.
BATCH SETUP: Cook in batches of 10–12 prawns maximum. Overcrowding drops pan temperature below 160°C and the prawns steam grey. For 40 covers, plan 4 sequential batches. Have trays ready for resting.

PRAWNS: Bring pan with chorizo fat to high heat. Add 10 ml olive oil. Once shimmering and just smoking, add garlic slices — 20 seconds until pale gold, not brown. Add chilli — 10 seconds. Add prawns in single layer. Cook 60–75 seconds first side without moving — you need a sear, not a steam. Flip. Cook 45–60 seconds second side. Prawns are done when flesh is just opaque at the thickest point and tail curls to a loose C — a tight curl means overcooked.

PAN SAUCE: Deglaze with 30 ml lemon juice per batch. Scrape fond. Remove pan from direct flame, mount with 15 g cold butter in pieces, swirl to emulsify into a short, glossy pan sauce. Return chorizo to pan. Toss once to coat. Taste and season with sea salt.

SERVE IMMEDIATELY: Spoon one prawn per Chinese soup spoon with one piece of chorizo, a single rocket leaf, a few drops of pan sauce, and a pinch of parsley. Lemon wedge on the side of the tray, not on each spoon.
service: This is a fire-to-pass canape. There is no hold. Prawns become rubbery within 4 minutes of cooking — do not pre-cook and hold warm.

Two-person execution: one chef manages the pan and batches, second person spoons onto spoons and passes. Establish a clear signal between kitchen and floor — pass when full tray of 10 is ready, not piecemeal.
Service window per batch: 8–10 minutes including cook time. Four batches across a 35–40 minute canape run is achievable on one 6-burner ring with a wide sauté pan.

Chorizo can be rendered up to 2 hours ahead and held at room temperature — re-crisp briefly in the pan during prawn cook. Garlic and chilli can be prepped and held chilled. Prawns must be stored in the coldroom and pulled batch by batch.
note: The two most common failures: wet prawns (steaming, not searing — grey colour, no crust, watery texture) and overcrowding (same effect). Both are temperature management problems. A 6-burner running one wide pan per ring can produce 80 prawns simultaneously across two pans if needed, but only with a second operator managing both pans. At 40 covers with a 2-person team, single-pan batching is the correct call. Chorizo fat is the umami spine of this dish — do not discard it. The butter mount is a small step that converts a flat lemon deglaze into a sauce that coats and carries the flavour.

name: Slow Cooked Beef Albondigas
subtitle: Spanish Meatballs · Smoked Paprika & Passata Sauce · Shaved Manchego
label: Beef Albondigas

ingredients.prep: 15–20% fat content — do not use extra lean, will produce dry, crumbling balls
fat adds binding and moisture — same fat ratio requirement
from day-old white bread, crusts removed — not panko
to soak breadcrumbs — panade prevents protein contraction and dry texture
meatball mix — divided from sauce quantity
fine chop — half in mix, half reserved for finish
meatball mix — weigh, do not eyeball

ingredients.item: Dry Sherry or Dry White Wine

ingredients.prep: shaved on a mandoline or peeler — not grated

method_steps: PANADE: Combine fresh breadcrumbs and milk in a small bowl. Allow to soak 10 min until fully absorbed. This panade step is critical — it introduces a starch-moisture matrix into the mince that physically prevents excessive protein crosslinking during cooking. Without it, meatballs tighten and dry even in a braise.

MEATBALL MIX: Combine beef mince, pork mince, soaked panade, beaten eggs, microplaned garlic, 3 tsp smoked paprika, cumin, half the parsley, weighed salt, and white pepper. Mix with hands until just combined — do not overwork. Overworking develops gluten from the breadcrumbs and toughens the mix. Fry off a small test portion in the pan and taste for seasoning before rolling all balls.

ROLL: Portion with a 40g scoop for consistency. Roll between palms with light pressure. Refrigerate rolled balls on a lined tray for 20 min before searing — chilling firms them and reduces crumbling in the pan.

SEAR: Heat 30 ml olive oil in a wide heavy-based pot or rondeau over medium-high heat. Sear meatballs in batches of 10–12 — do not crowd. Colour on all sides, approximately 4–5 min per batch. The Maillard crust is structural as well as flavour — it prevents the balls from disintegrating during the braise. Transfer to a tray. Discard excess rendered fat, leave 15 ml in pan.

SAUCE: In the same pot, sweat onion in remaining oil over medium heat 6–8 min until completely soft and translucent — no colour. Add sliced garlic, cook 2 min. Add 3 tsp smoked paprika and chilli flakes, stir 60 seconds until fragrant — bloom the spice in fat before adding liquid. Deglaze with sherry or wine, scraping the fond. Reduce by half, approximately 2 min. Add passata, stock, bay leaf. Stir, bring to a simmer. Taste — sauce should be savoury, slightly smoky, with a clean acid finish from the wine.

BRAISE: Nestle meatballs into sauce in a single layer if possible. Cover with a cartouche and tight-fitting lid. Transfer to oven at 160°C and braise for 30–35 min. Do not boil — aggressive heat causes the protein to contract and the balls to tighten. Gentle oven braise gives even, surrounding heat. After 30 min, remove lid and cook a further 10 min to reduce and concentrate sauce.

FINISH: Remove bay leaf. Taste sauce and adjust — add a few ml of sherry vinegar if acid is flat, season with sea salt. Sauce should coat a spoon lightly but still flow. If too thick, add a splash of stock. Scatter reserved parsley through.
service: Buffet: transfer to GN 1/1 with lid or chafing dish. Hold in bain marie at 75°C with sauce covering meatballs — exposed balls dry on the surface within 20 min.

Individual plated: 3–4 meatballs per portion, ladle sauce generously, shave manchego at the pass using a peeler, scatter parsley. Manchego must be applied at pass — it dissolves and goes greasy if it sits in the hot sauce.

Batch ahead: albondigas improve with resting. Cook the day before, refrigerate in sauce. Reheat gently in a covered pot at 150°C for 25 min or in a bain marie over 45 min. Sauce will tighten overnight — correct with a small addition of stock on reheat.
note: The panade (milk-soaked breadcrumbs) is the single most important technical step in this recipe — do not substitute with dry breadcrumbs added directly. The milk hydrates the starch which forms a gel network that dilutes myosin density, keeping the texture light and moist through the braise. Sherry or dry white wine is not optional — passata alone produces a flat, one-dimensional sauce. The acid from the wine interacts with the fat-soluble compounds in the smoked paprika to extend and lift the flavour. Manchego at service only: it is a finishing cheese, not a melting cheese — applying it hot kills its character. Passata is correct over Napoli for this application — it is a simpler, less herb-forward base that lets the smoked paprika and cumin lead.

name: Heirloom Tomato Bruschetta
subtitle: Charred Sourdough · Goats Cheese · Basil
label: Tomato Bruschetta

ingredients.prep: sliced 1.5cm thick, charred both sides on grill or grill pan
mixed colours, fine dice — drain excess liquid after dicing
leaves torn — add immediately before assembly
brought to room temperature 30 min before service

method_steps: TOMATO MIX: Fine dice heirloom tomatoes. Transfer to a colander over a bowl and season with salt — allow to drain for 10 min to remove excess liquid. Combine drained tomatoes with microplaned garlic, extra virgin olive oil, and red wine vinegar. Season with salt flakes and cracked pepper. Rest at room temperature for 15 min. Add torn basil immediately before assembly.

CROSTINI: Brush sourdough slices with olive oil on both sides. Char on a hot grill pan or 6-burner grill plate over high heat — approximately 1–2 min per side until grill marks are defined and bread is crisp but not brittle. Cool on a wire rack.
ASSEMBLE: Spread a generous layer of room-temperature goats cheese onto each crostini (approximately 10–12 g). Spoon tomato mix on top, allowing a little to tumble over the edge.
FINISH: Finish with a few salt flakes and a fine drizzle of extra virgin olive oil immediately before passing.
service: Assemble no more than 10 min before passing — crostini soften rapidly once topped.
Hold tomato mix separately in a chilled container until assembly. Drain again if mix has released more liquid while holding.
note: FLAG — KUSCHI EXCLUSION: Heirloom tomatoes are the key ingredient in this recipe. Tomatoes are on the Kuschi exclusion list. This item requires review before confirmation. Suggested substitutes: roasted capsicum and caramelised onion mix on goats cheese crostini; or whipped goats cheese with pickled shallots and fresh herb oil. Confirm with event coordinator before producing.

name: Greek Style Pulled Beef
subtitle: Slow Braised Chuck · Lemon, Oregano & Warm Spice · Tzatziki
label: Greek Pulled Beef

ingredients.prep: cut into 300–400 g pieces, sinew trimmed, surface moisture patted dry — brisket acceptable but chuck holds pull texture better
juice of 3 (approximately 90 ml), zest of 2 — add zest at the end of braise, not the beginning
good quality — this becomes the jus
rub half into beef before searing, reserve half for sauce

method_steps: SEASON & SEAR: Season beef pieces generously with salt, cracked pepper, and half the dried oregano — do this at least 30 min before searing, ideally overnight refrigerated, to allow the salt to penetrate and begin drawing surface moisture back in. Pat dry before searing. Heat olive oil in a rondeau or wide heavy pot over high heat until shimmering. Sear beef pieces in batches of 2–3 — do not crowd. Develop deep, dark Maillard colour on all faces, 2–3 min per face. This caramelised crust is the primary flavour contribution to the braising jus. Set beef aside on a tray.

AROMATIC BASE: Reduce heat to medium. In the same pot, sweat onion, celery, and garlic in remaining fond and fat 8–10 min until softened and beginning to colour. Add cinnamon, allspice, and remaining oregano. Stir 60 seconds to bloom spices in fat.

DEGLAZE & BUILD: Add white wine, scraping all fond from the base — this is the flavour layer that built during searing. Reduce wine by half, approximately 3–4 min. Add beef stock, lemon juice, rosemary sprigs, and bay leaves. Bring to a simmer. Taste the braising liquid before adding beef — it should be well-seasoned, fragrant, and clearly acidic. Correct now, not after the braise.

BRAISE: Return seared beef to the pot. Liquid should come two-thirds up the sides of the meat — top up with water or stock if needed. Cover tightly with a cartouche pressed onto the surface and a lid. Place in oven at 155°C. Braise 3.5–4 hours. At the 3-hour mark, check by probing the thickest piece — it should offer no resistance and begin to yield under fork pressure. Do not rush with higher heat — connective tissue (collagen) converts to gelatin progressively from around 80°C internal. It requires sustained time at temperature, not higher heat. Higher heat will cause the exterior to dry and fibres to tighten before the interior has converted.

REDUCE JUS: Remove beef to a tray, cover with foil. Strain braising liquid into a clean pot. Skim fat from the surface. Bring to a simmer and reduce by approximately one-third until it coats a spoon lightly and carries a rich, glossy body — the dissolved gelatin is providing viscosity. Add lemon zest now. Taste and correct with red wine vinegar in 5 ml increments until the acid is present and balancing the richness. Season with salt.

PULL: Using two forks, pull beef into large shreds — not fine mince. Chunks of 3–4 cm give good texture on the plate and in a buffet scoop. Return pulled beef to reduced jus. Toss gently to coat. Taste the combined beef and jus — season if needed.
HOLD: Pulled beef in jus holds well. For service, transfer to GN or bain marie and hold at 75°C. Keep covered to prevent surface drying.
service: Buffet: ladle beef and jus together — the jus is the sauce, there is no separate component. Tzatziki served separately in a labelled bowl beside the GN or dotted at pass from a piping bag or squeeze bottle.

Plated: approximately 150 g pulled beef per portion, spoon jus over, two dots of tzatziki alongside, scatter parsley. Tzatziki must not be mixed into the jus — the contrast of hot braised beef against cold, creamy tzatziki is the point of the dish.

Lemon zest was added late in the braise reduction — this is intentional. Zest added at the start of a 4-hour braise becomes bitter as the volatile aromatics break down into phenolic compounds. Added at the reduce stage, the bright citrus note survives to service.
Pairs with flatbread, steamed rice, or roasted chat potatoes. Do not pair with chips — the jus penetrates the crust immediately and makes them soggy.
note: Two technical points for the brigade: (1) Lemon zest timing — always add zest after pulling, during the jus reduction. It is aromatic and volatile. Extended braising converts the bright compounds into bitter ones. (2) Collagen-to-gelatin conversion is the mechanism that makes pulled beef work — it is a time-and-temperature function, not a temperature-alone function. Chuck needs sustained time above 75°C internal (ideally 82–88°C for full collagen conversion) at low ambient heat. This is why 155°C oven over 3.5 hours outperforms 180°C over 2 hours — the exterior does not tighten before the interior softens. Brisket is an acceptable substitute for chuck but will be fattier — trim visible fat cap to 4–5 mm before braising, not to zero. The fat renders into the jus and improves body.

name: Chargrilled Lemon & Thyme Chicken
subtitle: Marinated Thigh Fillet · Chargrilled · Lemon, Parsley & Caper Dressing
label: Lemon Thyme Chicken

ingredients.prep: skin-off, sinew trimmed, butterflied open if thick — even thickness ensures even grill time
juice of 3 (approximately 90 ml) for marinade, zest of 2 — juice of 1 reserved for dressing
leaves stripped — approximately 15 g stripped leaves
marinade — salt the marinade, it seasons from outside in
rinsed, roughly chopped — dressing, adds umami and brine note
fine chop — dressing, kept separate until service

method_steps: MARINADE: Combine lemon juice, lemon zest, thyme leaves, microplaned garlic, olive oil, chilli flakes, and salt. Whisk briefly to combine. Coat chicken thighs thoroughly — use a zip-lock bag or deep tray with cling film pressed to surface. Marinate minimum 4 hours refrigerated, overnight strongly preferred. Do not marinate beyond 18 hours — the acid in the lemon juice begins to denature surface proteins, producing a mushy exterior texture rather than a clean grill char.

GRILL PREP: Remove chicken from marinade 30 min before grilling and allow to come to room temperature. Shake off excess marinade — particularly garlic and thyme solids, which will burn on the grill before the chicken is cooked. Burnt garlic produces acrid bitterness that carries through the finished dish. Pat lightly with paper towel if needed.

GRILL: Bring chargrill or grill bars to high heat — surface temperature should be above 250°C. Brush bars lightly with oil. Place thighs presentation-side down. Grill 5–6 min without moving — moving prevents the Maillard crust from forming and causes sticking. You need clear grill marks and caramelisation before the first flip. Flip once. Grill second side 4–5 min. Target internal temperature 74°C at the thickest point. Thigh fillet is forgiving above 74°C — up to 78°C retains moisture due to higher fat and collagen content compared to breast. Do not cook above 80°C internal.
REST: Transfer to a wire rack over a tray. Rest 5 min loosely covered with foil. Do not stack — steam from stacking causes the crust to soften.

DRESSING: Whisk EVOO, white wine vinegar, and fresh lemon juice together. Add capers. Season with sea salt and cracked pepper. Dressing is made without parsley — parsley is added to the dressing only at the moment of service. Parsley wilts and oxidises within 10 minutes of contact with acid — pre-mixed dressing will be brown and bitter by service.

SLICE: Slice thighs on the bias against the grain into 3–4 pieces per thigh. This exposes the interior and allows the dressing to penetrate. For buffet, hold whole and slice at the station — sliced chicken dries faster.
service: Buffet: hold whole grilled thighs warm on a rack in a covered tray at 65°C in the kick oven. Slice at the station to order. Dress individual portions only — do not dress the whole tray. Add parsley to the dressing jug at service and stir immediately before spooning.
Plated: 1 thigh fillet sliced per portion, approximately 150 g. Spoon 20 ml of dressing over. Finish with a pinch of parsley and a few flakes of sea salt.
Capers in the dressing are the umami anchor replacing a missing savoury depth in the original herb dressing. Without them the dressing reads as flat and lemony — with them it carries and coats.
This dish holds significantly better than other proteins on this menu. Grilled thigh fillets can be held whole in the kick oven at 63°C for up to 45 min before quality degrades. Plan accordingly.
note: Two technical limits for the team: (1) Marinade time — the 18-hour ceiling on lemon-acid marinades is real. Citric acid hydrolyses surface myosin and actin chains progressively. Past 18 hours the exterior texture becomes mealy and does not form a proper crust on the grill. If overnight means 12 hours, that is fine. If it means 20 hours, pull the chicken out and rinse the surface. (2) Parsley in dressing — parsley oxidises rapidly in an acidic, oil-emulsified environment. The chlorophyll degrades to pheophytin, which is brown and slightly bitter. Mix parsley into the dressing only at the point of service. Keep dressing base and chopped parsley in separate containers in the coldroom and combine to order. This is a 10-second step that makes the difference between a bright green dressing and a brown one.

name: Duo of Cannoli
subtitle: Pistachio Cream · White Chocolate Ricotta · Twice-Dipped Crisp Shell
label: Duo Cannoli

ingredients.prep: medium, pre-made — check for cracks before service, store airtight until fill
drained overnight in muslin over a colander in the coldroom; weigh after draining — target 400 g drained yield
sifted, for white chocolate ricotta filling
chopped, melted over bain marie to 45°C, cooled to 28°C before folding
100% pure, unsweetened — taste before use, adjust sugar if paste is sweetened
whipped to firm soft peaks — 35–37% fat, well chilled before whipping
toasted at 160°C for 8 min, cooled, coarsely crushed — garnish for white choc ricotta ends
tempered shards or finely chopped block — garnish for pistachio cream ends
fleur de sel preferred — brightens both fillings
zest only — fold into white chocolate ricotta filling to cut richness

method_steps: RICOTTA DRAIN — OVERNIGHT: Place ricotta in muslin-lined colander over a bowl. Cover and refrigerate overnight minimum 8 hours. Weigh drained ricotta before use — you need 400 g. Discard whey. If ricotta is still wet, press lightly for 30 min more. Wet ricotta will collapse the filling within minutes of piping.

WHITE CHOCOLATE RICOTTA: In a cold bowl, beat drained ricotta with 80 g sifted icing sugar and 1 g fine salt until smooth — do not aerate excessively. Fold in cooled white chocolate (28°C) in two additions. Add lemon zest. Fold through 125 ml of the whipped cream in two stages using a spatula — cut and fold, do not stir. Filling should be smooth and hold a ribbon. Taste — adjust sweetness. Transfer to piping bag fitted with 12 mm plain nozzle. Refrigerate until needed.

PISTACHIO CREAM: In a separate bowl, whisk pistachio paste with 40 g sifted icing sugar and 1 g fine salt until combined. Fold through remaining 125 ml whipped cream in two stages — same fold technique. Do not over-fold; the cream must remain aerated. Taste — pistachio paste varies in intensity and salt content. Transfer to piping bag fitted with 12 mm plain nozzle. Refrigerate until needed.
SHELL CHECK: Inspect all 40 shells for cracks. Discard any compromised. Do not fill more than 20 min before service — shells soften rapidly once in contact with moisture from the filling.

FILL SHELLS: Pipe white chocolate ricotta into 20 shells from both ends simultaneously, meeting in the middle — overfill slightly so filling crowns 2–3 mm proud at each end. Repeat with pistachio cream in remaining 20 shells. Wipe any smears from the shell exterior with a clean cloth.

GARNISH — ENDS: Dip white choc ricotta ends into crushed toasted pistachios, pressing lightly to adhere. Dip pistachio cream ends into white chocolate shards. Work quickly — filling at the ends is the adhesive. Arrange on parchment-lined tray in a single layer.

HOLD: Filled cannoli hold maximum 15 min at room temperature, 25 min in the coldroom (cold dulls crunch). Prioritise plating as close to pass as possible. Do not stack — shells crack and garnish transfers.
GARNISH PREP (ADVANCE): Toast pistachios and prepare shards up to 24 hours ahead, store in separate airtight containers at room temperature.
service: Plate on slate or white rectangular plate — alternate one white choc ricotta and one pistachio per portion for visual contrast.
Dust lightly with icing sugar at pass using a fine sieve — do not blast with sugar, just a whisper.
Serve immediately after dusting. Call the pass the moment shells are filled.
Two-person team: one pipes and garnishes, one plates and dusts. Establish a rhythm — fill 4, plate 4, pass.
note: COUNCIL NOTES — Ricotta moisture is the single failure point in this dish. Under-drained ricotta will produce a wet, loose filling that softens the shell in under 5 min and pools on the plate. Weigh after draining — every time, every service. White chocolate quantity has been reduced from 80 g to 90 g and the melting temperature specified: chocolate added too hot will thin the ricotta and delay setting. Lemon zest added to the white choc ricotta is non-negotiable — without acid the filling is cloying and flat. Pistachio paste quality varies wildly — pure unsweetened Bronte paste is ideal; sweetened compound paste requires sugar reduction. Whipped cream should be firm soft peaks, not stiff — stiff cream will cause the pistachio filling to split when folded. Shell fill window is 15–20 min maximum; this must be communicated clearly at briefing. For 80-cover service, stagger filling in batches of 20.

name: Strawberry Ricotta Cheesecake
subtitle: Digestive Butter Base · Ricotta & Cream Cheese Filling · Macerated Strawberry
label: Ricotta Cheesecake

ingredients.prep: crushed to fine crumb — no large pieces, use food processor or rolling pin in bag
melted, cooled slightly — not hot or crumb will fry
add to base crumb mix — base needs seasoning
drained overnight in muslin in coldroom — target 800 g drained yield; weigh after draining
full fat, at room temperature minimum 1 hour — cold cream cheese causes lumps and over-beating
at room temperature — cold eggs cause batter to seize
zest of both, juice of 1 (30 ml) — zest into batter, juice reserved for compote
15 g — stabilises the set, reduces cracking risk on a ricotta-heavy batter
hulled, quartered — extra quantity to account for trim loss and tasting
for macerated strawberries — deepens flavour, optional but recommended

method_steps: RICOTTA DRAIN — OVERNIGHT: Place ricotta in muslin-lined colander over a bowl. Cover and refrigerate minimum 8 hours. Target yield 800 g drained. Discard whey. Wet ricotta produces a loose, curdled-looking bake that will not set cleanly.

BASE: Combine biscuit crumb, melted butter, and 2 g fine salt. Mix until crumb resembles wet sand — squeeze a handful; it should hold when pressed. Press firmly and evenly into base of lined 1/1 GN tray or into individual 8 cm rings to 5 mm depth. Use the base of a smaller tray or flat-bottomed measure to compact. Refrigerate 30 min minimum — not 20 min. An unchilled base will slip when batter is poured.

FILLING: In a stand mixer with paddle attachment, beat room-temperature cream cheese and caster sugar on medium speed until completely smooth — no lumps, approx 3 min. Scrape bowl. Add drained ricotta and beat on low just until combined — 30 seconds. Add eggs one at a time on lowest speed, incorporating fully between each addition. Add vanilla, lemon zest, 30 ml lemon juice, and 15 g cornflour sifted in. Mix on lowest speed until just combined — 20 seconds. Batter should be smooth and pourable. Do not over-beat; excess air causes the top to dome and crack during baking.

WATER BATH SETUP: Wrap the base of your baking vessel tightly in two layers of heavy-duty foil if using a springform or individual rings — water ingress ruins the base. Place in a deep roasting tray. Pour batter over chilled base. Fill roasting tray with boiling water to reach halfway up the sides of the cheesecake vessel.

BAKE: Conventional oven at 155°C (not 160°C — ricotta is more delicate than pure cream cheese). Bake 50–60 min for a full tray, 30–35 min for individual rings. The cheesecake is ready when the edges are set and there is a 5 cm diameter wobble in the centre when the tray is nudged — not sloshing, a slow jelly-wobble. Do not bake to fully set; it will continue cooking out of the oven.

COOL: Turn oven off. Prop door open with a wooden spoon — 10 cm gap. Leave cheesecake in the oven for 30 min. Then remove from water bath and cool to room temperature on a wire rack — minimum 1 hour. Transfer to coldroom. Refrigerate minimum 4 hours, overnight preferred before portioning. Rapid cooling causes cracking.
PORTION: For tray bake — use a hot dry knife (dip in hot water, wipe clean between cuts) to portion into 20 equal pieces. For rings — unmould cold, plate direct.

MACERATED STRAWBERRIES: Combine quartered strawberries, 50 g caster sugar, 25 ml lemon juice, and balsamic if using. Toss gently. Macerate at room temperature 20–30 min — not longer or berries collapse and release excess liquid. Taste: adjust lemon or sugar. Drain excess liquid before plating or it will pool on the plate and make the base soggy.
HOLD: Baked cheesecake holds 3 days covered in coldroom. Macerated strawberries: prepare fresh daily, maximum 1 hour ahead of service — do not hold overnight.
service: Plate cheesecake portion to order. Spoon macerated strawberries alongside or over the top — not pooled underneath or the base softens.
Dust lightly with icing sugar at pass.
Plate should be dry — wipe any strawberry juice from the rim before sending.
For plated service at 20 covers: pre-portion cheesecake in coldroom, hold on lined tray. Plate strawberries and dust at pass — two-person team can turn 20 covers in under 4 min.
note: COUNCIL NOTES — Two structural problems in the original: ricotta drain quantity was understated (900 g raw to yield 800 g drained) and oven temperature at 160°C is too aggressive for a ricotta-dominant batter — 155°C prevents premature protein seize on the ricotta. Cornflour added at 15 g stabilises the set without compromising texture — standard in Italian-style cheesecakes. Eggs and cream cheese must be at room temperature; cold dairy causes lumps that cannot be beaten out without over-aerating. Balsamic in the strawberry macerations is optional but recommended — it adds depth that lemon alone cannot achieve. Drain macerated strawberry liquid before plating or the base becomes compromised mid-service. Base salt was missing from the original — it is essential for contrast against the sweet filling.

name: Riviera Tiramisu
subtitle: Espresso-Soaked Savoiardi · Pasteurised Zabaglione · Mascarpone · Hazelnut Biscotti
label: Tiramisu

ingredients.prep: store in airtight container until assembly — humidity softens them before soaking
brewed and cooled to room temperature — minimum 6 shots double espresso, or moka pot; instant is not acceptable
Marsala preferred for depth; Kahlúa acceptable — add to cooled espresso soak
fresh, at room temperature — from 60 g eggs
additional — whisked into zabaglione during cooking for flavour and to assist pasteurisation
at room temperature — cold mascarpone splits when folded into warm zabaglione
35% fat minimum, whipped to firm soft peaks — well chilled bowl and whisk
sifted — dust generously; skimping looks amateurish
to serve alongside — one per portion
a pinch into mascarpone cream — elevates coffee and chocolate notes

method_steps: ESPRESSO SOAK: Brew espresso and cool completely to room temperature — hot soak will pre-cook the savoiardi unevenly. Combine 700 ml cooled espresso with 100 ml Marsala or liqueur in a shallow dish or hotel pan wide enough to dip the biscuits lengthways.

ZABAGLIONE — PASTEURISED METHOD: Combine 6 egg yolks, 150 g caster sugar, and 30 ml Marsala in a heatproof bowl. Whisk over a bain marie — water simmering, not boiling; bowl must not touch the water. Whisk continuously and vigorously until mixture reaches 82°C on a probe thermometer — this is the pasteurisation point. The mixture will triple in volume, turn pale yellow, and fall from the whisk in a thick ribbon that holds for 3 seconds. Do not rush with high heat; scrambled zabaglione cannot be recovered. Remove from heat. Continue whisking off the heat for 2 min to begin cooling. Transfer bowl to an ice bath and whisk until the zabaglione cools to below 30°C — approximately 8–10 min. Do not skip ice bath cooling; warm zabaglione melts the mascarpone.

MASCARPONE CREAM: Add room-temperature mascarpone and a pinch of fine salt to the cooled zabaglione. Fold with a spatula in large strokes — 8–10 folds, no more. Mixture should be smooth and homogenous. Fold in whipped cream in two additions using the same technique. Final cream should be thick, aerated, and hold a soft mound. If the cream looks curdled or grainy, the zabaglione was too warm or the mascarpone too cold — do not proceed; the texture will not recover.

ASSEMBLY — TRAY: Use a full 1/1 GN tray (65 mm deep) or individual glasses/portions. Working quickly and in batches: dip each savoiardi lengthways into the espresso soak — 1.5 seconds per side, no longer. They should be saturated but not collapsing. Lay in a single even layer, breaking biscuits to fill gaps if needed. Spread half the mascarpone cream evenly over biscuit layer — minimum 15 mm depth. Repeat with a second biscuit layer, then remaining cream. Smooth the top with a palette knife. Cream should reach the rim of the vessel.

SET: Cover with cling film directly on the surface of the cream — prevents a skin and cocoa migration. Refrigerate minimum 6 hours, overnight strongly preferred. The mascarpone cream needs time to firm around the soaked savoiardi — less than 4 hours produces a sloppy portion that does not hold when plated.

COCOA DUST: Dust with sifted Dutch-process cocoa at the point of plating only — never in advance. Cocoa applied early absorbs moisture from the cream and turns muddy and bitter. Use a fine-mesh sieve, held 20 cm above the surface, dust in two passes for even coverage.
PORTION: For tray bake — use a hot dry knife, wiping between cuts. Each portion: approximately 8 cm x 5 cm. Cut 4 x 5 grid for 20 portions. For glasses — scoop with a warmed spoon.
service: Plate individual portions or glasses. Dust cocoa at pass — never pre-dust.
Place hazelnut biscotti to the side of or leaning against the portion — not on top where it will absorb moisture from the cream.
For high-volume service: pre-cut portioned tray in coldroom, plate onto chilled plates at pass, dust cocoa, add biscotti, send immediately.
Chilled plates are important — tiramisu softens fast on a warm plate at room temperature service.
note: COUNCIL NOTES — The original method omitted a pasteurisation temperature for the zabaglione, which is a food safety issue in a professional kitchen. Egg yolks over a bain marie without a target temperature is not sufficient — the mixture must reach 82°C to be considered pasteurised. This has been corrected with a probe thermometer step. Espresso quantity increased from 600 ml to 700 ml — 500 g of savoiardi requires adequate soak volume for a full double-layer tray; running short mid-assembly means dry biscuit in the second layer. Mascarpone must be at room temperature; cold mascarpone added to even a properly cooled zabaglione will cause the cream to seize and grain. Minimum set time increased from 4 hours to 6 hours — 4 hours produces a soft, borderline unportionable result at service. Cocoa dusting must be at pass only, never in advance — this is non-negotiable. Hazelnut biscotti positioning corrected: do not place on top of the cream.

name: Kids Snack Pack
subtitle: Cheese · Crackers · Fruit · Veggies
label: Kids Snack Pack

ingredients.prep: cut into 2cm cubes or batons — approx 20g per pack
washed, cut into small bunches of 5–6 grapes — halve for under-5s
cut into 8cm batons, approx 4 per pack
cut into 8cm batons, approx 3 per pack — substitute for cucumber (Kuschi exclusion)

ingredients.item: Divided containers or sectioned snack boxes

method_steps: PREP: Portion all components per pack — 20g cheese, 15g crackers, one small bunch grapes, 2–3 strawberry halves, 4 carrot batons, 3 celery batons.
PACK: Divide into sectioned containers, keeping components separate — cheese and crackers together, fruit together, vegetables together. Do not mix wet and dry components.
LABEL: Label each pack clearly with contents and allergens (dairy). If made for a specific dietary requirement, mark accordingly.
service: Serve chilled — remove from coldroom no more than 20 min before service.
Packs can be prepared up to 4 hours ahead and held in the coldroom. Do not freeze.
note: FLAG RESOLVED — KUSCHI EXCLUSION: Raw cucumber has been replaced with celery batons. Cucumber is on the Kuschi exclusion list. Sugar snap peas are also a suitable alternative if celery is unavailable. Grapes must be halved for children under 5 — choking hazard. No added seasoning on kids components.

name: Kids Spaghetti Bolognese
subtitle: Beef Bolognese · Spaghetti · Parmesan
label: Kids Spaghetti
method_steps: BOLOGNESE: Heat olive oil in a wide heavy-based pot over high heat. Brown mince in batches — do not crowd the pan. Season as it cooks. Remove browned mince and set aside. Reduce heat to medium. Sweat onion, garlic, and carrot in residual oil for 5–6 min until softened. Add tomato paste and cook out for 2 min, stirring. Return mince to pot. Add passata and beef stock. Stir to combine. Simmer uncovered over low heat for 30 min, stirring occasionally, until sauce is reduced and rich. Season with salt and pepper — keep mild, no chilli.

PASTA: Bring a large pot of heavily salted water to a rolling boil. Cook spaghetti to al dente per packet time — typically 8–10 min. Reserve 100 ml pasta water before draining. Drain and do not rinse.
COMBINE: Toss drained spaghetti through bolognese sauce over low heat. Loosen with reserved pasta water as needed — sauce should coat the pasta without pooling.
PORTION: Serve 120 g pasta with 100 g bolognese per kids portion. Top with grated parmesan.
service: Serve immediately after combining. Bolognese holds well in a bain marie at 75°C for up to 1 hour.
For batch service: portion pasta into oiled trays, top with bolognese. Reheat at 170°C in conventional oven covered with foil for 10 min. Finish with parmesan at pass.
note: Kids portions — 120g pasta, 100g bolognese per serve. Mild seasoning only, no chilli. Parmesan is a dairy allergen — confirm with event coordinator if any dietary restrictions apply. Bolognese improves made a day ahead; reheat gently and adjust consistency with a splash of stock.

name: Bacon & Egg Muffins
subtitle: English Muffin · Bacon · Fried Egg · Cheddar
label: Bacon Egg Muffins

ingredients.prep: baked in rings for batch — or fried to order
condiment — serve on side or apply at assembly

method_steps: MUFFINS: Split English muffins and spread cut sides with softened butter. Toast cut-side down on a flat grill or in a conventional oven at 180°C for 5–6 min until golden. Hold warm.
BACON: Grill or pan-fry streaky bacon over medium-high heat until crispy. 2 rashers per muffin. Drain on absorbent paper. Hold warm.

EGGS — BATCH METHOD: Lightly grease egg rings and place on a flat grill or oven tray. Crack one egg into each ring. Season with salt and pepper. Bake at 175°C in conventional oven for 8–10 min until whites are just set and yolk is still slightly runny, or cook to well-done per event requirement. Remove rings carefully.
ASSEMBLE: Bottom muffin half, sauce, 2 rashers bacon, baked egg, slice or pinch of cheddar, top muffin half. Press gently to compact.
HOLD: Wrap each assembled muffin individually in foil. Hold in kick oven at 70°C for up to 20 min.
service: Serve warm. Remove foil at the pass or leave wrapped for guests to open — clarify with event brief.
For platter service, halve each muffin through the centre and arrange cut-side up on the board.
note: Baking eggs in rings is the preferred batch method — consistent diameter fits the muffin and keeps yolks centred for presentation. Season eggs in the ring before baking. Do not exceed 20 min hold time in foil — egg and bacon quality drops noticeably beyond this.

name: Bacon & Egg Paninis
subtitle: Pressed Panini · Bacon · Egg · Cheese · Chipotle
label: Bacon Egg Paninis

ingredients.prep: coarsely grated or thinly sliced — ensures even melt
brush on roll exterior before pressing

method_steps: CHIPOTLE MAYO: Blitz mayonnaise, chipotle in adobo, and lemon juice until smooth. Season. Hold chilled. Note: same batch as breakfast wraps — make one combined quantity if running both items.
BACON: Grill streaky bacon over medium-high heat until crispy. 2 rashers per panini. Drain on absorbent paper.
EGGS: Whisk eggs with a pinch of salt. Scramble in batches over low heat with a knob of butter, folding to large soft curds. Pull from heat while slightly underdone. Hold warm.
ASSEMBLE: Spread chipotle mayo on both cut faces of the roll. Layer 2 rashers bacon, a portion of scrambled egg, and cheddar on the base half. Close the roll. Brush exterior lightly with olive oil.

PRESS: Press on a panini grill or sandwich press over medium-high heat for 3–4 min until exterior is golden and marked, and cheese is melted. Do not press so hard that filling is forced out — moderate pressure only.
HOLD: Wrap each pressed panini individually in foil. Hold in kick oven at 70°C for up to 15 min.
service: Serve warm. Remove foil at the pass before plating.
For platter service, cut each panini diagonally and display cut-side up — shows layers.
note: Pressing is non-negotiable — it gives structure, melds the filling, and melts the cheese. Without pressing, the roll separates on the pass. Chipotle mayo is the same recipe as the breakfast wraps — consolidate into one batch to save prep time. Do not exceed 15 min hold time in foil after pressing.

name: Waffles
subtitle: Belgian Waffles · Maple Syrup · Berry Compote · Chantilly
label: Waffles

ingredients.item: Mixed Berry Compote — house or quality purchased

method_steps: BATTER: Sift flour, sugar, baking powder, salt into a large bowl. In a separate jug, whisk yolks, milk, melted butter, and vanilla until combined. Pour wet into dry and mix until just combined — a few lumps are fine. Do not overmix. Rest batter 5 min.
EGG WHITES: Whip whites in a clean bowl to stiff peaks. Fold through rested batter in 3 additions, using a large spatula — keep folds light to retain volume.

WAFFLES: Preheat waffle iron to medium-high (approx. 190°C if adjustable). Lightly spray or brush iron with melted butter between each waffle. Pour approx. 120–130 ml batter per waffle. Cook 3–4 min until deep golden, steam has mostly subsided, and waffle releases cleanly. Do not open iron early.
CHANTILLY: Whip cream, icing sugar, and vanilla in a cold bowl to soft-firm peaks — should hold shape but remain spoonable. Refrigerate until service.
HOLD: If holding, place cooked waffles in a single layer on a wire rack in a 160°C conventional oven, uncovered. Do not stack.
SERVE: Waffle topped with berry compote, a generous spoonful of Chantilly, and maple syrup drizzled to order.
service: Serve immediately for best crispness — waffles lose texture within 5 min off the iron.
If holding for service, keep waffles uncovered on a wire rack in a 160°C oven for no more than 15 min.
Chantilly, compote, and maple syrup portioned separately and applied at the pass.
note: Folded egg whites are non-negotiable for a light, crisp waffle — do not skip. Rest batter 5 min before cooking to allow baking powder to activate. Yield assumes approx. 120–130 ml batter per waffle and a standard Belgian iron.

name: Creamy Basil Pesto Pasta Salad
subtitle: Fusilli · Basil Pesto · Bocconcini · Pine Nuts · Parmesan
label: Pesto Pasta Salad

ingredients.item: Basil Pesto — house or quality purchased

ingredients.prep: loosens and enriches the pesto dressing
toasted in dry pan until golden, cooled
leaves picked, larger leaves torn — finish only

method_steps: PASTA: Cook in a large pot of heavily salted boiling water (water should taste seasoned) until al dente — typically 1 min less than packet time. Drain well. Immediately toss hot pasta with EVOO to prevent sticking. Spread on a tray and cool completely to room temperature.

PESTO CREAM: Whisk pesto, cream, and lemon juice together until smooth. Season with salt and white pepper. Consistency should coat a spoon — loosen with a touch more cream if needed. Refrigerate until service.
COMBINE: Within 1 hour of service, toss cooled pasta with pesto cream until evenly coated. Fold in bocconcini gently.
FINISH: At service, scatter toasted pine nuts, torn basil, and parmesan shavings across the top. Do not mix in — keep as a visible garnish.
service: Serve at room temperature or lightly chilled (no colder than 15°C — cold dulls the pesto flavour).
Combine pesto cream and pasta within 1 hour of service only — pasta dressed too early will absorb dressing and dry out.
Pine nuts, basil, and parmesan applied at the pass to maintain texture and colour.
note: Do not dress pasta ahead of time. Pesto oxidises and loses colour — combine no more than 1 hour before service. Bocconcini should be at room temperature before folding in — cold bocconcini seizes the dressing.

name: Moroccan Couscous Salad
subtitle: Couscous · Dried Apricot & Sultana · Herbs · Harissa Dressing · Toasted Almonds
label: Moroccan Couscous

ingredients.prep: boiling — use vegetable stock to keep vegan
toasted at 170°C for 5–6 min until golden, cooled

method_steps: COUSCOUS: Place couscous in a deep tray or bowl. Pour boiling vegetable stock over, stir once, cover tightly with cling film or a lid. Rest 5 min. Uncover and fluff thoroughly with a fork, breaking up any clumps. Stir in olive oil and season with salt. Spread on a tray to cool to room temperature.
DRESSING: Whisk lemon juice, EVOO, harissa, cumin, and cinnamon together until combined. Season with salt and pepper. Taste — dressing should be bold and slightly sharp.
COMBINE: Toss cooled couscous with dressing, dried apricots, sultanas, most of the parsley, mint, and spring onion. Mix well and check seasoning. Refrigerate if making ahead.
FINISH: At service, scatter toasted almonds and remaining fresh herbs over the top. Do not mix almonds through — keep on top for crunch.
service: Serve at room temperature or lightly chilled.
If made ahead, loosen with an extra squeeze of lemon juice and a drizzle of EVOO before service — couscous absorbs dressing overnight.
Toasted almonds applied at service only to maintain crunch.
note: Best made the day prior — flavours develop significantly overnight. Vegan confirmed: use vegetable stock only. Cinnamon quantity reduced to ½ tsp to avoid overpowering the harissa. Almonds must stay off the salad until service.

name: Pumpkin, Feta & Pepita Salad
subtitle: Roast Pumpkin · Danish Feta · Toasted Pepitas · Honey Lemon Dressing · Mixed Leaves
label: Pumpkin Feta Salad

ingredients.prep: toasted in dry pan until lightly popped, cooled

method_steps: PUMPKIN: Toss diced pumpkin with olive oil, cinnamon, smoked paprika, and salt until evenly coated. Spread in a single layer on lined oven trays — do not overcrowd. Roast at 200°C (fan-forced) for 25–30 min, turning once at 15 min, until edges are golden and caramelised. Cool to room temperature on trays before assembling.
DRESSING: Whisk honey, lemon juice, Dijon, and a pinch of salt until combined. Slowly whisk in EVOO to emulsify. Adjust seasoning.
ASSEMBLE: Spread mixed leaves across the base of the platter or bowl. Arrange roast pumpkin over leaves. Crumble feta across, scatter pepitas.
DRESS: Drizzle dressing over the assembled salad at the point of service — not before.
service: Dress at the point of service only — leaves wilt rapidly once dressed.
Pumpkin can be roasted the day prior and held in coldroom; bring to room temperature before assembling.
Pepitas applied last to preserve crunch.
For buffet service, keep dressing in a separate jug on the side.
note: Do not dress ahead. Roast pumpkin is an excellent prep-ahead component — roast the day before and hold in coldroom. Dice size increased to 3 cm (from 2 cm) to hold shape through roasting and avoid mushiness. Pepitas must go on at service.

name: Traditional Garden Salad
subtitle: Mixed Leaves · Shaved Vegetables · House Vinaigrette
label: Garden Salad

ingredients.prep: peeled, shaved on mandoline or peeler into long ribbons
topped and tailed, halved on the bias

method_steps: VINAIGRETTE: Whisk Dijon, vinegar, honey, and a pinch of salt together until combined. Slowly drizzle in EVOO while whisking continuously to form a stable emulsion. Season with salt and pepper. Store in a sealed jar or squeeze bottle — shake well before use.
PREP: All vegetables must be completely dry before assembly. Spin leaves in batches if needed. Pat capsicum dry. Hold prepped veg separately in coldroom until service.
ASSEMBLE: Combine leaves and spinach as the base. Scatter shaved carrot, capsicum julienne, snow peas, and spring onion evenly across the top.
DRESS: Toss with vinaigrette immediately before service. Season lightly after dressing.
service: Dress at service only — pre-dressed salad wilts within 10–15 min.
For buffet service, present vinaigrette in a separate jug or squeeze bottle for self-service.
All vegetables must be bone-dry before dressing — any water breaks the emulsion and dilutes flavour.
note: Vinaigrette yield approx. 130 ml — sufficient for 20 portions dressed to coat, not drench. Prep leaves and vegetables separately in coldroom and combine only at service. Snow peas can be blanched briefly (30 sec, iced) for a slightly more tender result if preferred.

name: Creamy Potato, Bacon & Herb Salad
subtitle: Waxy Potato · Crispy Bacon · Herb Mayo · Chives
label: Potato Bacon Salad
method_steps: POTATOES: Place whole unpeeled potatoes in a pot of cold well-salted water. Bring to the boil, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook until just tender — a skewer meets slight resistance in the centre, approx. 20–25 min depending on size. Do not boil hard or the skins split. Drain and cool 10 min.
SLICE: While still warm, slice kipflers on the bias into 1.5 cm rounds, or halve desiree. Skin on throughout.

BACON: Lay rashers flat on lined oven trays. Bake at 200°C for 12–15 min until deeply golden and crispy. Cool on a wire rack, then crumble into rough pieces. Do not press into small crumbs — keep texture.
DRESSING: Whisk mayonnaise, sour cream, vinegar, and Dijon together until smooth. Season generously with salt and pepper — dressing should be well-seasoned as it will be absorbed by the potatoes.

COMBINE: While potatoes are still warm (not hot), toss with dressing until evenly coated — warm potatoes absorb dressing for better flavour. Fold in spring onion and most of the chives and parsley. Taste and adjust seasoning.
FINISH: Scatter remaining fresh herbs and crumbled bacon across the top at service. Do not mix bacon through ahead of time.
service: Serve at room temperature — ideally within 4 hours of dressing.
Dress potatoes while warm for maximum flavour absorption, then hold at room temperature or lightly chilled (above 10°C).
Bacon applied at service only to maintain crispness — pre-mixed bacon will go soft.
note: Kipfler gives the best waxy texture and holds shape well when sliced. Desiree is a reliable backup. Do not overcook — potato must hold its shape once sliced. Bacon quantity converted from '20 rashers' to approx. 400 g for accurate prep. Dress warm, serve at room temp.

name: Spinach & Ricotta Pastizzi Platter
subtitle: Maltese Pastizzi · Spinach & Ricotta · Warm
label: Pastizzi Platter

ingredients.prep: frozen or fresh from quality supplier

method_steps: BAKE: Arrange pastizzi on lined baking trays with space between each — do not crowd. Bake from frozen at 190°C (conventional oven) for 22–25 min until pastry is deep golden, puffed, and crisp. If baking fresh, reduce time to 15–18 min. Check base — it should be firm, not soft.
REST: Remove from oven and rest on tray for 3 min before plating — filling retains significant heat and can burn.
PLATE: Arrange on a lined platter or board. Finish with a very light scatter of flaked sea salt. Place lemon wedges around the edge of the platter.
service: Serve warm. If holding, transfer to kick oven at 70°C on a wire rack — maximum 20 min hold before quality drops.
For longer events, bake in batches rather than holding all pieces — pastry softens on a platter within 15–20 min.
Lemon wedges on the side; do not squeeze over platter pre-service.
note: Pastizzi cool and soften quickly once plated — batch baking and staggered service is strongly recommended for events over 30 min. Supplier quality is critical. Do not refreeze and rebake.

name: Mixed Box Hot Nibbles
subtitle: Spring Rolls · Dim Sims · Cocktail Sausage Rolls · Arancini
label: Hot Nibbles Box

ingredients.prep: dipping sauce — for dim sims and spring rolls

method_steps: SEQUENCE: Stagger cooking so all items are hot simultaneously. Start sausage rolls first (longest cook), then fry items. Target a 5-min window where all items finish together.
SAUSAGE ROLLS: Place on a lined tray. Bake at 190°C (conventional oven) for 15–18 min until pastry is golden and base is firm. Do not fry.
SPRING ROLLS: Fry in oil preheated to 180°C for 3–4 min until deep golden and crispy. Drain on a wire rack — not paper towel, which traps steam and softens the base.
DIM SIMS: Fry at 175°C for 4–5 min until golden, or steam in a bamboo steamer over boiling water for 8–10 min. Do not mix methods on the same batch. Drain well.
ARANCINI: Fry at 180°C for 3–4 min until golden and heated through. Alternatively bake at 200°C for 12–15 min, turning once — spray lightly with oil before baking for colour.
HOLD: Transfer all cooked items to lined trays on a wire rack in the kick oven at 70°C. Do not stack. Hold for no more than 15 min — crispness deteriorates beyond this.
service: Arrange by type in a lined box or on a platter — keep each item in its own section. Label each type for service.
Dipping sauces in individual ramekins placed alongside or at the edge of the platter.
Serve promptly — fried items lose crispness rapidly. Do not cover platter with cling film or foil as steam will soften pastry.
For longer events, cook in staggered batches of 10 every 15–20 min rather than holding all 40 pieces.
note: Cook each item in the correct medium — spring rolls and arancini fry better than they bake; sausage rolls must bake. Fried and baked items must be cooked on separate trays and in separate equipment. Coordinate timing to bring all items to temperature simultaneously. Sauce quantities standardised to approx. 30 ml per ramekin — adjust ramekin count to event size.

name: House Focaccia
subtitle: Sourdough · Thyme · Rosemary · Grapes
label: House Focaccia

ingredients.prep: quantity to be confirmed by head chef
tapas topping — halved or left whole

method_steps: BLOOM: In a large mixing bowl or container, combine 30 g dry yeast, 40 g honey and 300 g active sourdough starter. Mix briefly and allow to bloom for 10–15 minutes until the yeast is active and the mixture is bubbling.

DOUGH: Add 2400 g bread flour, 2300 ml lukewarm water and 100 g olive oil to the bloomed starter. Mix by hand or with a dough hook until a shaggy dough forms with no dry flour remaining. Do not overwork at this stage.
FIRST RISE: Cover the dough and allow to rest at room temperature for 45 minutes.

STRETCH & FOLD #1: Wet your hands. Working around the container, stretch each side of the dough up and fold it over the centre — four folds per round. Rotate the container 90° between each fold. Perform 3–4 rounds of stretch and fold with a few minutes rest between each.
SECOND REST: Cover and rest at room temperature for a further 45 minutes.
STRETCH & FOLD #2: Repeat the stretch and fold process as above — 3–4 rounds. The dough should feel smoother and more elastic than after the first round.
COLD PROOF: Cover the dough tightly and refrigerate overnight (minimum 10 hours, maximum 18 hours). The dough will continue to develop flavour and structure in the cold.

NEXT MORNING — PAN: Generously coat shallow gastronorm bains with olive oil — do not be shy, the oil is structural and creates the crust. Remove dough from the fridge. Portion and fold the dough into the oiled bains. Do not stretch or force it flat — it will relax and fill the bain on its own. Allow to rest and rise at room temperature for 30 minutes.

DIMPLE & TOP: With oiled fingers, dimple the dough firmly and evenly across the entire surface. For the tapas version, press in fresh thyme sprigs, rosemary and whole or halved grapes into the dimples. Drizzle with olive oil.
BAKE — STAGE 1: Cover bains tightly with foil or lids. Bake at 220°C (conventional) for 30 minutes. The steam trapped inside creates an open, airy crumb.
BAKE — STAGE 2: Remove covering. Reduce oven to 180°C. Bake uncovered for a further 30 minutes until the top is deep golden and the edges are crisp and pulling away from the bain.
COOL: Remove from bains immediately and place on a cooling rack. Do not rest in the bain — the bottom will sweat and the crust will soften. Allow to cool for at least 20 minutes before slicing.
service: Slice into portions on the board. For tapas service, cut into generous rectangles approx. 8 × 5 cm.
Serve warm or at room temperature. Can be lightly toasted on the flat grill or kick oven to order.
For polpette service: slice 2 cm thick and grill both sides until golden.
note: Salt quantity not included in the provided recipe — confirm with head chef before production. Standard for this flour weight would be approximately 45–50 g salt flakes added with the flour. Two-stage bake (covered then uncovered) is essential — covered stage builds the crumb, uncovered stage builds the crust. Do not skip the cooling rack step.
